- `input(title, placeholder?)` — Show a text input dialog

These should work in command handlers too since `ExtensionCommandContext` extends the same UI capabilities.

2. **Curated skills** from Composio (development-relevant subset only)

Now let me write a conversion script that processes all VoltAgent subagents:

arguments.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Convert VoltAgent subagents to OMP agent definitions.

- Adds category prefix to names
- Maps model tiers (opus/sonnet/haiku) to ollama-cloud models
- Maps Claude Code tool names to OMP tool names
- Preserves system prompt content
"""

import os
import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

VOLT_REPO = Path("/tmp/volt-subagents/categories")
OUTPUT_DIR = Path(os.path.expanduser("~/.omp/agent/agents"))

CATEGORY_MAP = {
    "01-core-development": "dev",
    "02-language-specialists": "lang",
    "03-infrastructure": "infra",
    "04-quality-security": "qual",
    "05-data-ai": "data",
    "06-developer-experience": "dx",
    "07-specialized-domains": "spec",
    "08-business-product": "biz",
    "09-meta-orchestration": "meta",
    "10-research-analysis": "res",
}

MODEL_MAP = {
    "opus": "ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro",
    "sonnet": "ollama-cloud/glm-5.1",
    "haiku": "ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b",
}

TOOL_MAP = {
    "Read": "read",
    "Write": "write",
    "Edit": "edit",
    "Bash": "bash",
    "Glob": "find",
    "Grep": "search",
    "WebFetch": None,  # not available in subagents
    "WebSearch": None,
    "Task": "task",
}

def convert_frontmatter(content: str, category_prefix: str, filename: str) -> str:
    """Convert VoltAgent frontmatter to OMP format."""
    # Parse frontmatter
    fm_match = re.match(r'^---\n(.*?)\n---\n(.*)$', content, re.DOTALL)
    if not fm_match:
        print(f"  WARNING: No frontmatter in {filename}, skipping")
        return None
    
    fm_text = fm_match.group(1)
    body = fm_match.group(2)
    
    # Parse frontmatter fields
    name = None
    description = None
    tools = []
    model = None
    
    for line in fm_text.split('\n'):
        line = line.strip()
        if line.startswith('name:'):
            name = line.split(':', 1)[1].strip().strip('"').strip("'")
        elif line.startswith('description:'):
            description = line.split(':', 1)[1].strip().strip('"').strip("'")
        elif line.startswith('tools:'):
            tools_str = line.split(':', 1)[1].strip()
            tools = [t.strip() for t in tools_str.split(',') if t.strip()]
        elif line.startswith('model:'):
            model = line.split(':', 1)[1].strip().strip('"').strip("'")
    
    if not name:
        # Derive from filename
        name = filename.replace('.md', '')
    
    if not description:
        description = f"Specialized agent for {name}"
    
    # Apply prefix
    omp_name = f"{category_prefix}-{name}" if category_prefix else name
    
    # Map model
    omp_model = MODEL_MAP.get(model, MODEL_MAP["sonnet"]) if model else MODEL_MAP["sonnet"]
    
    # Map tools - always include essential OMP tools
    omp_tools = ["read", "write", "edit", "bash", "find", "search"]
    for t in tools:
        mapped = TOOL_MAP.get(t)
        if mapped and mapped not in omp_tools:
            omp_tools.append(mapped)
    
    # Build OMP frontmatter
    omp_fm = f"""---
name: {omp_name}
description: "{description}"
model: {omp_model}
tools: [{', '.join(omp_tools)}]
---"""
    
    return f"{omp_fm}\n{body}"


def main():
    OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    
    total = 0
    converted = 0
    
    for cat_dir in sorted(VOLT_REPO.iterdir()):
        if not cat_dir.is_dir() or cat_dir.name.startswith('.'):
            continue
        
        # Skip READMEs
        cat_prefix = CATEGORY_MAP.get(cat_dir.name)
        if not cat_prefix:
            print(f"SKIP: Unknown category {cat_dir.name}")
            continue
        
        print(f"\nCategory: {cat_dir.name} -> {cat_prefix}")
        
        for md_file in sorted(cat_dir.iterdir()):
            if not md_file.name.endswith('.md') or md_file.name == 'README.md':
                continue
            
            total += 1
            
            content = md_file.read_text()
            converted_content = convert_frontmatter(content, cat_prefix, md_file.name)
            
            if converted_content is None:
                continue
            
            # Output filename: category-prefix-originalname.md
            orig_name = md_file.stem
            out_name = f"{cat_prefix}-{orig_name}.md"
            out_path = OUTPUT_DIR / out_name
            
            out_path.write_text(converted_content)
            converted += 1
            print(f"  {md_file.name} -> {out_name}")
    
    print(f"\nConverted {converted}/{total} agents")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

The system is complete. Here's a summary of everything that was built:

---

## Xpert System — Installed and Ready

### What was built

**144 specialized subagents** converted from VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents, organized into 10 categories with category-prefixed names and mapped to your ollama-cloud model tiers:

| Category | Prefix | Count | Example agents |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core Development | `dev-` | 11 | `dev-fullstack-developer`, `dev-api-designer` |
| Language Specialists | `lang-` | 30 | `lang-rust-engineer`, `lang-python-pro`, `lang-typescript-pro` |
| Infrastructure | `infra-` | 16 | `infra-docker-expert`, `infra-devops-engineer` |
| Quality & Security | `qual-` | 16 | `qual-code-reviewer`, `qual-debugger` |
| Data & AI | `data-` | 13 | `data-ai-engineer`, `data-llm-architect` |
| Developer Experience | `dx-` | 14 | `dx-documentation-engineer`, `dx-mcp-developer` |
| Specialized Domains | `spec-` | 13 | `spec-embedded-systems`, `spec-fintech-engineer` |
| Business & Product | `biz-` | 12 | `biz-product-manager`, `biz-technical-writer` |
| Meta-Orchestration | `meta-` | 11 | `meta-multi-agent-coordinator`, `meta-task-distributor` |
| Research & Analysis | `res-` | 8 | `res-research-analyst`, `res-competitive-analyst` |

**Model tiers**:
- `ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro` — 25 agents needing heavy reasoning (architecture review, security audit, etc.)
- `ollama-cloud/glm-5.1` — 102 agents for balanced work (your default model)
- `ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b` — 17 agents for fast/light tasks (documentation, research)

**15 curated skills** from Composio (development-relevant subset, not the 500+ SaaS integrations):
`nixify`, `mcp-builder`, `skill-creator`, `webapp-testing`, `content-research-writer`, `theme-factory`, `changelog-generator`, `file-organizer`, `image-enhancer`, `developer-growth-analysis`, `langsmith-fetch`, `docx`, `pdf`, `pptx`, `xlsx`

**Skill-agent pairings** — 8 key agents reference relevant skills in their system prompts (e.g., `lang-rust-engineer` references `skill://nixify`, `dev-frontend-developer` references `skill://theme-factory`).

**`/xpert` extension** — interactive command with:
- `/xpert` — opens category selector, then agent selector
- `/xpert list` — lists all 144 agents by category
- `/xpert skills` — lists all 15 skills
- `/xpert activate <name>` — loads an agent's expertise into context
- `/xpert skill <name>` — loads a skill into context

### How to use

1. **Browse experts**: Type `/xpert` to get an interactive category selector
2. **Load expertise**: Select an agent to inject its system prompt into your session
3. **Spawn subagent**: Use `task` with `agent: "lang-rust-engineer"` to delegate work
4. **Activate skills**: Type `/xpert skill mcp-builder` or `/skill:nixify`
5. **Nixify**: Type `/skill:nixify` in any project directory to generate flake.nix + .envrc

All agents and skills are `alwaysApply: false` — they only consume context when you explicitly invoke them.

content.text: All tasks complete. The system is fully built and installed. Restart omp to pick up the new agents, skills, and extension.

Create a slash command called update-agents and update-skills that will check the repositories for changes and adapt the newly added/improved agents and skills to our custom format. The idea will be to keep a local file where we keep track of what agents and tools we have taken from those repositories to avoid re-reading the full repositories each time. I also want a system to update the models used in the agents. I think that the best way would be to have a configuration file where I detail all available models in ollama cloud that I may want to use, and tag each of them and explain their strenghts and best usage scenarios. And then for each of the agents we would choose the best model for that agent. The idea is to have a cohort of models, instead a one-model-for-all, and that I will be updating that configuration when new models come up and are available, substituing the old ones and thus continuously improving everything, not only the models but also the agents

This is a substantial system design task. Let me break it down into components:

1. **Model catalog** — A config file describing all available ollama models with tags, strengths, and best-use scenarios
2. **Agent-to-model mapping** — Each agent references a model role (not a hardcoded model ID), and the config maps roles to actual models
3. **Manifest file** — Tracks which agents/skills were sourced from which repos, at what commit, so we can incrementally update
4. **Update commands** — `/update-agents` and `/update-skills` that pull repo changes and adapt new/changed items

Let me first study the current state, then design and build.

Good — I can see the current state. The model field is hardcoded to specific ollama-cloud model IDs. The plan:

1. **`~/.omp/agent/models.yml`** — Model catalog with tags, strengths, and role assignments
2. **`~/.omp/agent/manifest.yml`** — Tracks sourced agents/skills with repo commit hashes
3. **`~/.omp/agent/extensions/xpert/index.ts`** — Updated to include `/update-agents`, `/update-skills`, and model role resolution
4. **Agent frontmatter** — Changed from `model: ollama-cloud/glm-5.1` to `model: {role}` where role maps to the model catalog

# Xpert Model Catalog
#
# This file defines all available ollama-cloud models, their capabilities,
# and which model role each agent type should use.
#
# Model roles are abstract identifiers (e.g., "heavy-reasoning", "balanced")
# that map to concrete model IDs. When you add or remove models, update the
# role mappings below. Agents reference roles, not model IDs.
#
# To update: add new models under [models], then update the [roles] section
# to point roles at the best available model for that tier.
#
# After editing this file, run: /update-agents reconcile
# This rewrites all agent frontmatter to use the current role→model mapping.

[models]

Title: Best Ollama Models in 2026: Full Comparison, Storage, RAM, Pros and Cons - Hyaking

URL Source: https://www.hyaking.com/best-ollama-models-2026-comparison/

Published Time: 2026-04-13T10:12:33+05:30

Markdown Content:
If you want to run AI models locally, **Ollama** is one of the easiest ways to do it. It lets you install models on your own machine, run them from the terminal, call them through a local API, and connect them to tools like VS Code or Codex. On macOS, Ollama officially supports **macOS Sonoma or newer**, works on **Apple M-series Macs**, stores models under `~/.ollama`, and warns that models can take **tens to hundreds of GB** of disk space.

The problem is that there is no single “best” Ollama model for everyone. The right choice depends on whether you care most about **coding**, **general reasoning**, **image understanding**, **speed**, or **low storage use**. Ollama’s current model library makes that tradeoff pretty clear: some models are tiny and fast, some are coding specialists, some are multimodal, and some are so large they only make sense on very high-end systems.

## Short answer: which Ollama model is best?

For most people in 2026, these are the most practical picks:

*   **Best Ollama model for coding:**`qwen3-coder:30b`
*   **Best Ollama model for reasoning and agent workflows:**`gpt-oss:20b`
*   **Best smaller multimodal model:**`gemma4:latest`
*   **Best compact general model:**`qwen3:8b`
*   **Best compact Microsoft text model:**`phi4:latest`
*   **Best vision-first local model:**`llama3.2-vision:11b`

## Ollama model comparison table

| Model | Best for | Approx. download size | Context window | Input type | My practical verdict |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `qwen3-coder:30b` | Serious coding and repo work | 19GB | 256K | Text | Best local coding pick |
| `gpt-oss:20b` | Reasoning, tools, agent workflows | 14GB | 128K | Text | Best balanced reasoning model |
| `gemma4:latest` | Smaller multimodal use | 9.6GB | 128K | Text, Image | Best balanced small multimodal |
| `gemma4:26b` | Stronger multimodal reasoning | 18GB | 256K | Text, Image | Better than `latest` if hardware allows |
| `qwen3:8b` | Fast, lightweight local use | 5.2GB | 40K | Text | Good starter model |
| `phi4:latest` | Compact text reasoning | 9.1GB | 16K | Text | Fine, but context is short |
| `llama3.2-vision:11b` | Image understanding | 7.8GB | 128K | Text, Image | Good vision option |
| `llama3.3:70b` | Large multilingual text model | 43GB | 128K | Text | Strong, but heavy |
| `gpt-oss:120b` | Very large local reasoning | 65GB | 128K | Text | Too heavy for most laptops |
| `qwen3-coder:480b` | Extreme local coding setup | 290GB | 256K | Text | Only for very large-memory systems |

The size, context, and input data above come from Ollama’s official model tag pages and model library pages.

## 1) Qwen3-Coder 30B: the best Ollama model for coding

If your main goal is **coding**, `qwen3-coder:30b` is the model I would recommend first. Ollama describes Qwen3-Coder as Alibaba’s long-context model family for **agentic and coding tasks**, and the `30b` build is listed at **19GB** with a **256K context window**. The model page also says it is optimized for repository-scale understanding and highlights real-world software engineering and execution-driven reinforcement learning.

### Pros

*   Best fit here for backend and frontend development
*   Long 256K context is much better for bigger repos
*   Stronger coding focus than general chat models

### Cons

*   Text-only, not multimodal
*   Heavier than smaller models like Qwen 8B or Gemma 4
*   Still not a replacement for a top hosted frontier model in every situation

### Best for

Use it if you work on **Django, Angular, React, Node.js, Python, Java, or repo-scale code changes** and want a strong local coding assistant.

## 2) GPT-OSS 20B: the best Ollama model for reasoning and agent workflows

`gpt-oss:20b` is one of the best all-round local reasoning choices on Ollama right now. Ollama describes the GPT-OSS family as open-weight models designed for **powerful reasoning, agentic tasks, and versatile developer use cases**. The official Ollama tag page lists `gpt-oss:20b` at **14GB** with a **128K context window**, and the model page says MXFP4 quantization helps the smaller model run on systems with as little as **16GB memory**.

### Pros

*   Strong reasoning and tool-oriented positioning
*   Good balance of size and capability
*   Good fit for structured outputs, tool use, and developer workflows

### Cons

*   Text-only
*   Not as code-specialized as Qwen3-Coder
*   The `120b` variant is far less practical on normal laptops

### Best for

Pick it when you want a **local general-purpose assistant** with better reasoning than tiny models and a better chance of handling tool-style workflows.

## 3) Gemma 4: the best smaller multimodal Ollama model

`gemma4:latest` is a strong choice when you want a **smaller local model that can also understand images**. Ollama says Gemma 4 models are **multimodal**, support **text and image input**, and are designed for **reasoning, agentic workflows, coding, and multimodal understanding**. The `latest` tag is **9.6GB** with a **128K context window**, while `gemma4:26b` is **18GB** with a **256K context window**.

### Pros

*   Multimodal out of the box
*   Smaller than many larger reasoning models
*   Good balance of quality, local usability, and context window

### Cons

*   Not as code-specialized as Qwen3-Coder
*   `latest` is a smaller generalist, not a dedicated coding specialist

### Best for

Choose Gemma 4 if you want **one local model for text, images, and general reasoning** without jumping to very large downloads.

## 4) Qwen 3 8B: the best lightweight starter model

`qwen3:8b` is a good pick when you want a model that is **easy to install, quick to run, and good enough for everyday local AI use**. The Ollama tag page shows the `8b-q4_K_M` build at **5.2GB** with a **40K context window**.

### Pros

*   Small and fast
*   Very practical for laptops
*   Good starter model for local testing

### Cons

*   Not ideal for large code changes
*   Much less headroom than `qwen3-coder:30b` or `gpt-oss:20b`

## 5) Phi-4: decent small text model, but with a short context window

`phi4:latest` is a compact Microsoft text model listed by Ollama at **9.1GB** with a **16K context window**. That short context window is the main reason I would rank it behind Qwen3-Coder, GPT-OSS, and Gemma 4 for most modern local workflows.

### Pros

*   Compact enough for many systems
*   Fine for shorter text-only tasks

### Cons

*   16K context is limiting in 2026
*   Not the best choice for large files, long chats, or repo-wide coding work

## 6) Llama 3.2 Vision and Llama 3.3: where they fit

If you need a local model for **image understanding**, `llama3.2-vision:11b` is a reasonable option. Ollama lists it at **7.8GB** with a **128K context window**, and its model page says it is optimized for visual recognition, image reasoning, captioning, and answering questions about images.

If you want a **larger multilingual text model**, `llama3.3:70b` is still relevant. Ollama lists it at **43GB** with a **128K context window**, and the model page says it is optimized for multilingual dialogue use cases.

## Storage requirements: how much disk space Ollama models need

One of the biggest mistakes people make is assuming Ollama itself is the thing that uses a lot of storage. The app is not the main issue. The real disk usage comes from the models you pull, and Ollama’s macOS docs explicitly warn that model storage can grow into the **tens to hundreds of GB**.

Here is a practical storage snapshot:

| Model | Storage needed |
| --- | --- |
| `qwen3-coder:30b` | 19GB |
| `gpt-oss:20b` | 14GB |
| `gpt-oss:120b` | 65GB |
| `gemma4:latest` | 9.6GB |
| `gemma4:26b` | 18GB |
| `qwen3:8b` | 5.2GB |
| `phi4:latest` | 9.1GB |
| `llama3.2-vision:11b` | 7.8GB |
| `llama3.3:70b` | 43GB |
| `qwen3-coder:480b` | 290GB |

These storage figures come from the official Ollama library and tag pages.

## RAM and hardware requirements: what you actually need

Storage size is not the same thing as RAM or unified memory. That matters a lot.

Ollama explicitly says:

*   `gpt-oss:20b` can run on systems with as little as **16GB memory** because of MXFP4 quantization
*   `gpt-oss:120b` is designed to fit on a **single 80GB GPU**
*   `qwen3-coder:480b` requires a minimum of **250GB of memory or unified memory** for local use

A practical buying guide looks like this:

| Hardware tier | Recommended Ollama models |
| --- | --- |
| 16GB RAM / unified memory | `qwen3:8b`, `phi4:latest`, some smaller Gemma variants |
| 24GB to 32GB | `gemma4:latest`, `gpt-oss:20b` |
| 32GB to 64GB | `qwen3-coder:30b`, `gemma4:26b`, `qwen3:30b` |
| 80GB-class GPU | `gpt-oss:120b` |
| 250GB+ memory / unified memory | `qwen3-coder:480b` |

The first and second rows are practical recommendations; the last three rows are directly supported by Ollama’s published model guidance.

## Step-by-step: how to install and use Ollama

### Step 1: Install Ollama

On macOS, Ollama’s preferred installation method is to mount the DMG and drag the app into the **Applications** folder. Ollama supports **macOS Sonoma (v14) or newer** and **Apple M-series Macs**.

### Step 2: Start Ollama

Use:

Ollama’s CLI reference lists `ollama serve` as the command to start Ollama.

### Step 3: Pull a model

Examples:

The CLI reference documents `ollama pull` for downloading a model.

### Step 4: Run a model

Examples:

The CLI reference documents `ollama run` as the basic way to run a model.

### Step 5: List installed models

Use:

or via the local API:

Ollama documents both the CLI list command and the `/api/tags` endpoint for listing models.

### Step 6: Use the local API

Example:

Ollama’s chat API is exposed at `POST /api/chat`, and the docs show requests to `http://localhost:11434/api/chat` with a `model` and `messages` array.

### Step 7: Add Ollama models to VS Code

Ollama’s VS Code integration docs say VS Code supports Ollama models through the built-in AI chat flow. The quick setup command is:

For manual setup, Ollama says to open the Copilot Chat sidebar, open the model settings, click **Add Models**, select **Ollama**, and then unhide the models. The docs also note that VS Code requires you to be logged in to use the model selector, though that does not require a paid Copilot plan.

### Step 8: Use Ollama with Codex

If you want local coding workflows with Codex, Ollama’s Codex integration docs say Codex works with Ollama and recommends a context window of at least **64K tokens**. The quick setup is:

Manual usage is:

and you can select a model with:

The docs also show profile-based configuration using `~/.codex/config.toml`.

## Which Ollama model should most people choose?

If you want a simple answer:

*   Choose **`qwen3-coder:30b`** if you mostly care about coding.
*   Choose **`gpt-oss:20b`** if you want a strong general reasoning model for local use.
*   Choose **`gemma4:latest`** if you want a lighter multimodal model.
*   Choose **`qwen3:8b`** if you want a fast and compact starter model.

## My final recommendation

For most developers, content creators, and local AI users in 2026:

*   **Best overall local coding model:**`qwen3-coder:30b`
*   **Best overall reasoning model:**`gpt-oss:20b`
*   **Best all-around small multimodal model:**`gemma4:latest`
*   **Best low-storage starter model:**`qwen3:8b`

If you are on a laptop, start smaller. If you are on a workstation or a high-memory Apple Silicon Mac, you can move up to the heavier models. But for most real users, the sweet spot is still around the **8B to 30B** range. That is where performance, storage, and usability finally start to balance out. This last recommendation is my practical judgment based on the official sizes, contexts, and published model positioning above.

Title: Ollama Cloud Models for Code Review - An Honest Comparison Using Real Examples

URL Source: https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e

Published Time: 2026-03-13T20:31:20Z

Markdown Content:
Recently, AI tools have become an important part of modern software development.

 Solutions such as Cursor, OpenAI Codex, and Claude Code allow developers to generate code, accelerate function writing, and automate routine tasks.

This significantly increases development speed. However, there is also a downside: code begins to appear faster than teams can properly review it.

As a result, the load on the **code review** process increases. This raises an important question: **can LLMs themselves help developers review code?**

In this article, I decided to test how well cloud models available through Ollama handle code review tasks and compare their responses on real Pull Requests.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#contents) Contents

*   [Goal of the Article](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#goal-of-the-article)
*   [Existing Solutions and Their Problems](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#existing-solutions-and-their-problems)
*   [Why Ollama Cloud?](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#why-ollama-cloud)
*   [Evaluation Criteria and Models](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#evaluation-criteria-and-models)
*   [Testing Conditions](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#testing-conditions)
*   [Test Pull Requests](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#test-pull-requests)
*   [Final Comparison Table](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#final-comparison-table)
*   [Conclusion](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#conclusion)

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#goal-of-the-article) Goal of the Article

The goal of this article is to evaluate how well modern LLMs available through Ollama can perform **high-quality code reviews** on real Pull Requests and identify which models deliver the best results.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#existing-solutions-and-their-problems) Existing Solutions and Their Problems

Before moving to the model tests, it is worth briefly looking at existing solutions, their limitations, and why Ollama was chosen for this experiment.

Today, several tools already exist for AI-powered code review, including CodeRabbit, Claude Code (review features), and Qodo.

These cloud services integrate with GitHub, GitLab, or IDEs and automatically analyze changes in Pull Requests.

Typically, such tools perform several main tasks:

*   analyze Pull Request diffs
*   detect potential bugs, security vulnerabilities, and bad practices
*   leave comments directly in the Pull Request
*   propose possible fixes

For example, **CodeRabbit** can act as a **“first reviewer”**, automatically checking code and pointing out potential problems before a human reviewer looks at the Pull Request.

These tools fit well into the developer workflow and can significantly speed up the code review process. However, most of them share several common limitations:

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#1-cost) 1. Cost

Most of these solutions are paid services, and the price can grow significantly as the number of Pull Requests increases.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#2-confidential-code-risks) 2. Confidential Code Risks

For companies working with NDA restrictions or sensitive data, there is always a risk when sending source code to external infrastructure. There is also limited transparency about whether the code might be used to train models.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#3-limited-customization) 3. Limited Customization

In many services, it is impossible to fully customize the model for a specific project — for example, by defining custom review rules, architectural constraints, or corporate coding guidelines.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#4-limited-analysis-context) 4. Limited Analysis Context

Models often analyze only the Pull Request diff and do not have access to additional context such as documentation, ADRs (Architecture Decision Records), or overall project architecture. This can reduce analysis depth and accuracy.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#5-lack-of-support-for-local-models) 5. Lack of Support for Local Models

Most of these solutions do not allow the use of custom or locally deployed models, limiting flexibility and experimentation with different LLMs.

Therefore, the main question is not only about price or infrastructure, but rather:

**Which model is actually capable of performing high-quality code review?**

This is exactly what I decided to test by comparing several models on real Pull Requests.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#why-ollama-cloud) Why Ollama Cloud?

A logical question arises: why choose Ollama Cloud for testing?

The main reason is that Ollama provides a convenient way to work with **multiple models simultaneously**, making it possible to compare different LLMs under identical conditions and obtain more objective results.

In addition, Ollama allows you to:

*   quickly switch between models
*   use both cloud and local LLMs
*   build custom tools on top of its API
*   experiment with different model configurations

Because of this flexibility, Ollama turned out to be a convenient platform for comparative testing of models in code review tasks.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#evaluation-criteria-and-models) Evaluation Criteria and Models

To evaluate the quality of AI code review, I used a **5-point scale** for each metric.

A total of **six metrics** were evaluated, covering different aspects of code analysis quality.

| Metric | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Accuracy** | How accurately the model finds real problems in the code |
| **Security awareness** | Ability to identify potential vulnerabilities and security risks |
| **Hallucination** | Tendency to invent problems that do not exist |
| **Depth** | Depth of analysis and understanding of the change context |
| **Practical fixes** | Whether the model proposes real and applicable fixes |
| **Human acceptance rate** | Portion of comments that developers would actually accept or apply |

For testing, I selected **three models** available through Ollama:

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#qwen-35) Qwen 3.5

Qwen 3.5 is a family of large language models developed by Alibaba and focused on programming tasks, reasoning, and AI agent development.

It is considered one of the strongest open LLMs in its class and is widely used for code generation and analysis.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#gptoss) GPT-OSS

GPT‑OSS is an open language model focused on programming and code analysis tasks.

 It is designed for building developer tools, automation systems, and AI code review workflows.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#deepseek-v31) DeepSeek v3.1

DeepSeek v3.1 is a large language model developed by DeepSeek for programming tasks, code analysis, and complex reasoning.

These models were selected because they are currently considered among the strongest LLMs for programming-related tasks. In addition, they are commonly used by my colleagues in everyday development workflows, making them interesting candidates for practical comparison.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#testing-conditions) Testing Conditions

To test real Pull Requests, I used my **four-year-old repository containing a legacy Python project**.

Each model had its **own Pull Request**, but **the same prompt** was used for all models to ensure a fair comparison.

All models also used a **RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)** tool to access additional context from project files when necessary.

All models were run under identical conditions with the same prompt and access to the same project context.

Configuration details for each model are shown below.

_(config blocks remain unchanged — they are already language-agnostic)_

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#test-pull-requests) Test Pull Requests

Now we move to the most interesting part — **practical testing of the models**.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#qwen-35) Qwen 3.5

Pull Request:

[https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action/pull/2](https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action/pull/2)

| Metric | Score | Comment |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Accuracy | 4/5 | The model identifies real problems in the code. Most comments are correctly linked to specific diff lines. |
| Security awareness | 3.5/5 | Some security risks are detected, but coverage is incomplete. No systematic vulnerability analysis. |
| Hallucination | 4/5 | Low tendency to invent issues. Comments are mostly based on actual code. |
| Depth | 3.5/5 | Analysis goes beyond simple lint-level review, but architectural context is limited. |
| Practical fixes | 4/5 | Some comments include concrete and applicable fixes. |
| Human acceptance rate | 4/5 | Most comments are constructive. Some are stylistic suggestions that developers might ignore. |

**Final score: 3.8**

Overall, the model performed surprisingly well. It not only identified issues but often suggested **concrete fixes**.

 However, the **depth of analysis could sometimes be higher**, and it would be beneficial if the model used available tools more actively to retrieve additional context.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#gptoss) GPT-OSS

Pull Request:

[https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action/pull/3](https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action/pull/3)

| Metric | Score | Comment |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Accuracy | 3/5 | Some findings are correct, but others are vague or generalized. |
| Security awareness | 3/5 | Basic security aspects detected but analysis remains shallow. |
| Hallucination | 2.5/5 | Occasionally makes assumptions about behavior without enough context. |
| Depth | 3/5 | Analysis is mostly local and fairly superficial. |
| Practical fixes | 3/5 | Fix suggestions are often general and lack concrete code. |
| Human acceptance rate | 3/5 | Some comments may be useful, but many may be ignored. |

**Final score: 2.9**

The result was **somewhat disappointing**. Many comments were generic and not particularly helpful for making concrete decisions about code improvements.

The model also rarely proposed **specific auto-fixes**, limiting the practical usefulness of the review.

On the positive side, the **comment style is clear and structured**, but overall the analysis lacks depth.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#deepseek-v31) DeepSeek v3.1

Pull Request:

[https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action/pull/5](https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action/pull/5)

**Note:** I reduced the final score by **1 point** because the model was unable to use tools without enabling _think mode_.

| Metric | Score | Comment |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Accuracy | 4.5/5 | Finds real problems and clearly explains the reasons. |
| Security awareness | 4/5 | Detects security risks effectively. |
| Hallucination | 4/5 | Low tendency to invent issues. |
| Depth | 4.5/5 | Provides deep analysis with explanations and consequences. |
| Practical fixes | 4.5/5 | Provides concrete fixes and sometimes code examples. |
| Human acceptance rate | 4/5 | Most comments would likely be accepted. |

**Final score: 3.25 (4.25)**

DeepSeek v3.1 delivered the **highest-quality analysis among the tested models**.

Comments were concise but informative. The model frequently explained problems, suggested improvements, and sometimes provided **direct auto-fix examples**, which is particularly useful for developers.

The main drawback was the need to enable **think mode** for proper tool usage.

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#final-comparison-table) Final Comparison Table

| Model | Accuracy | Security awareness | Hallucination | Depth | Practical fixes | Human acceptance rate | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Qwen 3.5 | 4 | 3.5 | 4 | 3.5 | 4 | 4 | 3.8 |
| GPT-OSS | 3 | 3 | 2.5 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2.9 |
| DeepSeek v3.1 | 4.5 | 4 | 4 | 4.5 | 4.5 | 4 | 3.25 (4.25) |

* * *

## [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#conclusion) Conclusion

Several observations can be made from the results.

First, **Ollama cloud models can indeed be used for code review tasks**, but the quality of results strongly depends on the chosen model and tool configuration.

Second, **context configuration and tooling are critical**. With proper setup (for example, RAG and access to project files), models better understand the codebase and provide more accurate recommendations.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#when-should-you-use-ollama-for-code-review) When should you use Ollama for code review?

*   when a project contains **sensitive code or NDA restrictions**
*   when code **must not leave company infrastructure**
*   when it is possible to run models **locally on powerful servers or workstations**

In these cases, local models may be a good alternative to SaaS solutions.

### [](https://dev.to/urlbug/ollama-cloud-models-for-code-review-an-honest-comparison-using-real-examples-2o7e#when-ollama-may-not-be-the-best-choice) When Ollama may not be the best choice

If security restrictions are not critical and budget is not an issue, specialized services such as CodeRabbit, Claude Review, or QoDo may provide **more stable out-of-the-box review quality**, since they use proprietary analysis pipelines and additional models.

Nevertheless, this experiment shows that with proper configuration and the right model selection, **LLMs can already be a useful assistant in the code review process**.

All experiments in this article were conducted using my CLI tool for AI-based code review — **CodeFox-CLI**, which allows connecting different models (not only Ollama), working with project context, and automating Pull Request analysis.

Tool:

[https://github.com/codefox-lab/CodeFox-CLI](https://github.com/codefox-lab/CodeFox-CLI)

Repository used in tests:

[https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action](https://github.com/codefox-lab/Demo-PR-Action)

arguments.content:
# Xpert Model Catalog
#
# This file defines all available ollama-cloud models with their capabilities,
# strengths, and best-use scenarios. Agent model assignments reference abstract
# "roles" (defined at the bottom), not concrete model IDs.
#
# When you add a new model or a model improves:
#   1. Add it under [models]
#   2. Update [roles] to point the best model for each role
#   3. Run: /update-agents reconcile
#      This rewrites all agent frontmatter to use current role→model mappings.
#
# When a model is retired or you want to stop using it:
#   1. Remove it from [models]
#   2. Update [roles] to point to a replacement
#   3. Run: /update-agents reconcile

[models]

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# TIER 1 — FRONTIER REASONING
# Best for: architecture, security audit, complex multi-step reasoning,
#           adversarial analysis, deep code understanding
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[models.deepseek-v4-pro]
id       = "ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro"
name     = "DeepSeek V4 Pro"
params   = "284B MoE (13B active)"
reasoning = true
thinking  = "effort"  # three modes: minimal/low/high
context   = 1048576
max_tokens = 384000
cost_input = 1.74
cost_output = 3.48
strengths = [
    "Deepest reasoning available on ollama-cloud",
    "Three-mode thinking: minimal for speed, low for balanced, high for depth",
    "1M token context — can hold entire large codebases",
    "Best-in-class for security audits, architecture review, adversarial analysis",
    "Excellent at multi-step logical proofs and complex refactoring plans",
    "Superior at understanding subtle invariants and cross-file dependencies",
]
weaknesses = [
    "Slowest and most expensive model",
    "Overkill for simple edits or formatting",
    "Large output can be verbose without tight prompts",

# Security audits, architecture reviews, adversarial analysis, formal verification
# Requires: deepest reasoning, large context, high accuracy

# Requires: deep reasoning, long context, instruction following

# ── Research & Synthesis ───────────────────────────────────────────────
# Literature review, competitive analysis, knowledge synthesis, formal specs
# Requires: knowledge breadth, long output, structured writing
research-synthesis        = "ollama-cloud/qwen3.5:397b"

# ── Agentic Engineering (DEFAULT) ──────────────────────────────────────
# Day-to-day coding, debugging, feature implementation, API design
# Requires: strong coding, tool use, multi-file editing, instruction following
agentic-engineering       = "ollama-cloud/glm-5.1"

# ── Code-Specialized Engineering ───────────────────────────────────────
# Language-specific coding: Rust, Go, Python, TypeScript, etc.
# Requires: best code generation, repo-scale understanding, multi-file edits
code-specialist           = "ollama-cloud/qwen3-coder-next"

# ── Large-Scale Code Editing ───────────────────────────────────────────
# Multi-file refactors, bulk edits, codebase exploration with agentic tools
# Requires: tool use, large context, multi-file editing
large-edit                = "ollama-cloud/devstral-2:123b"

# ── Long-Form Document Generation ──────────────────────────────────────
# Technical writing, specifications, reports, documentation, contracts
# Requires: very long output (100K+ tokens), structured formatting, consistency
long-document             = "ollama-cloud/minimax-m2.7"

# ── Fast Reasoning ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Quick analysis, structured output, moderate-complexity tasks, code review
# Requires: reasoning ability but faster than frontier models
fast-reasoning            = "ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-flash"

# ── Balanced Reasoning ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# General-purpose reasoning when you need thinking but not frontier depth
# Requires: good reasoning, decent speed, reasonable output length
balanced-reasoning        = "ollama-cloud/gpt-oss:20b"

# ── UI / Visual Analysis ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# UI/UX review, accessibility testing, screenshot analysis, design review
# Requires: multimodal (text + image), visual reasoning
visual-analysis           = "ollama-cloud/gemini-3-flash-preview"

# ── Lightweight Visual ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

# ── Fast Coding / Mechanical ───────────────────────────────────────────
# Formatting, scaffolding, simple edits, documentation stubs, linting
# Requires: speed, code understanding, no deep reasoning needed
fast-coding               = "ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b"

# ── Multi-Agent Orchestration ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Coordinating multiple agents, task delegation, workflow management

#
# Tracks which agents and skills were sourced from which repositories,
# at what commit, so we can incrementally update when repos change.

# Format:

#   date = "<iso-date>"
#   agents = ["<prefix-agent-name>", ...]
#   skills = ["<skill-name>", ...]
#

Now let me create the role-to-agent mapping and rewrite all agent frontmatter to use roles:

arguments.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Reconcile agent frontmatter model assignments from models.yml role mappings.

Reads ~/.omp/agent/models.yml for role→model mappings,
reads ~/.omp/agent/role-map.yml for agent→role assignments,
then rewrites each agent's frontmatter to use the correct model.
"""

import re
import sys
from pathlib import Path

AGENTS_DIR = Path.home() / ".omp" / "agent" / "agents"
MODELS_YML = Path.home() / ".omp" / "agent" / "models.yml"
ROLE_MAP_YML = Path.home() / ".omp" / "agent" / "role-map.yml"


def parse_yaml_simple(filepath: Path) -> dict:
    """Minimal YAML parser for our specific format. Handles [sections] and key = value pairs."""
    result = {}
    current_section = None
    for line in filepath.read_text().splitlines():
        stripped = line.strip()
        if not stripped or stripped.startswith("#"):
            continue
        section_match = re.match(r"^\[(\S+)\]", stripped)
        if section_match:
            current_section = section_match.group(1)
            result[current_section] = {}
            continue
        kv_match = re.match(r"^(\S+)\s*=\s*(.+)$", stripped)
        if kv_match and current_section:
            key = kv_match.group(1)
            value = kv_match.group(2).strip().strip('"').strip("'")
            result[current_section][key] = value
    return result


def parse_role_map(filepath: Path) -> dict:
    """Parse role-map.yml: agent-name = role"""
    result = {}
    for line in filepath.read_text().splitlines():
        stripped = line.strip()
        if not stripped or stripped.startswith("#"):
            continue
        kv_match = re.match(r"^(\S+)\s*=\s*(.+)$", stripped)
        if kv_match:
            agent = kv_match.group(1)
            role = kv_match.group(2).strip().strip('"').strip("'")
            result[agent] = role
    return result


def get_roles(models_yml: dict) -> dict:
    """Extract role→model_id from models.yml [roles] section."""
    return models_yml.get("roles", {})


def reconcile():
    if not MODELS_YML.exists():
        print(f"ERROR: {MODELS_YML} not found")
        sys.exit(1)

    models = parse_yaml_simple(MODELS_YML)
    roles = get_roles(models)

    if not ROLE_MAP_YML.exists():
        print(f"ERROR: {ROLE_MAP_YML} not found. Run generate-role-map first.")
        sys.exit(1)

    role_map = parse_role_map(ROLE_MAP_YML)

    updated = 0
    skipped = 0

    for agent_file in sorted(AGENTS_DIR.glob("*.md")):
        content = agent_file.read_text()
        agent_name = agent_file.stem

        # Find the role for this agent
        role = role_map.get(agent_name)
        if not role:
            print(f"  SKIP {agent_name}: no role mapping")
            skipped += 1
            continue

        # Resolve role to model ID
        model_id = roles.get(role)
        if not model_id:
            print(f"  SKIP {agent_name}: role '{role}' not found in models.yml")
            skipped += 1
            continue

        # Replace model: line in frontmatter
        new_content = re.sub(
            r"^(model:\s*).+$",
            f"\\g<1>{model_id}",
            content,
            count=1,
            flags=re.MULTILINE,
        )

        if new_content != content:
            agent_file.write_text(new_content)
            print(f"  UPDATED {agent_name}: {role} -> {model_id}")
            updated += 1
        else:
            print(f"  OK {agent_name}: already {model_id}")
            skipped += 1

    print(f"\nReconciled: {updated} updated, {skipped} unchanged")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    reconcile()

---

## Complete Xpert System

### 144 agents across 10 different models

| Model | Role | Agents | Best at |
|---|---|---|---|
| `qwen3-coder-next` | `code-specialist` | 46 | Language-specific coding (Rust, Go, Python, TS, etc.) |
| `glm-5.1` | `agentic-engineering` | 23 | Day-to-day coding, feature implementation, general dev |
| `deepseek-v4-flash` | `fast-reasoning` | 18 | Quick analysis, code review, debugging |
| `devstral-small-2:24b` | `fast-coding` | 13 | Formatting, scaffolding, simple edits |
| `deepseek-v4-pro` | `deep-audit` | 13 | Security audits, architecture review, compliance |
| `qwen3.5:397b` | `research-synthesis` | 9 | Research, knowledge synthesis, legal |
| `kimi-k2.6` | `architecture-planning` | 7 | System design, migration, complex planning |
| `nemotron-3-super` | `multi-agent` | 6 | Agent orchestration, task delegation |
| `gemini-3-flash-preview` | `visual-analysis` | 5 | UI/UX, accessibility, screenshot analysis |
| `minimax-m2.7` | `long-document` | 4 | Technical writing, specs, long-form docs |

### 15 specialized roles (not just "coding")

`deep-audit`, `architecture-planning`, `research-synthesis`, `agentic-engineering`, `code-specialist`, `large-edit`, `long-document`, `fast-reasoning`, `balanced-reasoning`, `visual-analysis`, `lightweight-visual`, `visual-reasoning`, `fast-coding`, `multi-agent`, `quick-thinking`, `trivial`

### Configuration files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `~/.omp/agent/models.yml` | Model catalog: 18 models with strengths/weaknesses + 16 role→model mappings |
| `~/.omp/agent/role-map.yml` | Agent→role assignments for all 144 agents |
| `~/.omp/agent/manifest.yml` | Source tracking: repo URLs, commit hashes, what was imported |
| `~/Projects/Personal/omp-test/reconcile-agents.py` | Reconciliation script that rewrites frontmatter from roles |

### Commands

| Command | Action |
|---|---|
| `/xpert` | Interactive selector: category → agent |
| `/xpert list` | List all 144 agents by category with roles |
| `/xpert models` | Show model catalog with roles and strengths |
| `/xpert skills` | List all 15 skills |
| `/xpert activate <name>` | Load agent expertise into session |
| `/xpert skill <name>` | Load skill into session |
| `/update-agents check` | Show source repo sync status |
| `/update-agents reconcile` | Rewrite all agent frontmatter from current `models.yml` + `role-map.yml` |
| `/update-skills check` | Show skill source status |

### How to evolve the system

1. **New model available on ollama-cloud**: Add it to `models.yml` under `[models]`, update the `[roles]` section to point the relevant role(s) at the new model, then run `/update-agents reconcile`
2. **Better model for a role**: Change one line in `models.yml` `[roles]`, run `/update-agents reconcile` — all agents with that role update at once
3. **New agents from VoltAgent repo**: Run `/update-agents pull` to fetch and convert
4. **New skills from Composio**: Run `/update-skills pull` to fetch and adapt
5. **Retire an old model**: Remove from `models.yml`, reassign its roles, run reconcile

summary:
## Goal
1. Create a "nixify" skill that analyzes a project and generates `flake.nix` + `.envrc` for NixOS development with direnv
2. Create an on-demand subagent/skill loading system: browse by category, load only what's needed, avoid context waste
3. Create `/update-agents` and `/update-skills` slash commands that incrementally sync from upstream repos
4. Create a model catalog (`models.yml`) where models are tagged with strengths/roles, and agents reference abstract roles instead of hardcoded model IDs — enabling continuous improvement as new models are added

## Constraints & Preferences
- User is on NixOS with nix 2.31.3 (flakes enabled) and direnv 2.37.1
- Default model: `ollama-cloud/glm-5.1`
- All agents/skills must have `alwaysApply: false` to avoid context waste
- Agents should use abstract model roles, not hardcoded model IDs
- A manifest file must track what was sourced from repos (commit hash) to enable incremental updates
- The model catalog should support tagging models with strengths and best-use scenarios
- User wants a "cohort of models" approach — different models for different agent types

## Progress

### Done
- [x] Created `~/.omp/agent/skills/nixify/SKILL.md` with comprehensive flake generation workflow (428 lines), detection heuristics, 4 strategy templates, direnv setup, verification steps
- [x] Created template files: `~/.omp/agent/skills/nixify/templates/{rust,python,node,go,c-cpp,multi,common-packages}.md`
- [x] Converted 144 VoltAgent subagents to OMP format at `~/.omp/agent/agents/*.md` with category prefixes (dev-, lang-, infra-, qual-, data-, dx-, spec-, biz-, meta-, res-)
- [x] Created 15 curated Composio skills at `~/.omp/agent/skills/*/SKILL.md` (mcp-builder, skill-creator, webapp-testing, content-research-writer, theme-factory, changelog-generator, file-organizer, image-enhancer, developer-growth-analysis, langsmith-fetch, docx, pdf, pptx, xlsx, nixify)
- [x] Created `~/.omp/agent/extensions/xpert/index.ts` with `/xpert` command (category selector → agent selector, `/xpert list`, `/xpert skills`, `/xpert activate <name>`, `/xpert skill <name>`)
- [x] Created `~/.omp/agent/extensions/xpert/package.json`
- [x] Added skill-agent pairings to 9 key agents (lang-rust-engineer, lang-python-pro, dev-frontend-developer, dx-documentation-engineer, dev-backend-developer, infra-devops-engineer, lang-typescript-pro, data-ai-engineer, lang-golang-pro)
- [x] Fixed stale `.omp` symlink (was pointing to deleted nix store path; replaced with `.omp.hm-backup` moved to `.omp`)
- [x] Created `~/.omp/agent/models.yml` with model catalog (13 models across 4 tiers) and 6 role mappings

### In Progress
- [ ] Create `~/.omp/agent/manifest.yml` — track sourced agents/skills with repo commit hashes for incremental updates
- [ ] Create `/update-agents` slash command in xpert extension — pull repo changes, diff against manifest, adapt new/changed agents
- [ ] Create `/update-skills` slash command in xpert extension — same for Composio skills
- [ ] Rewrite agent frontmatter to use role references (e.g., `model: balanced`) instead of hardcoded IDs (e.g., `model: ollama-cloud/glm-5.1`)
- [ ] Implement `/update-agents reconcile` to rewrite all agent model fields based on current role→model mapping from models.yml
- [ ] Test the complete update workflow

### Blocked
- No blockers currently

## Key Decisions
- **Agent naming**: Category prefix convention (e.g., `lang-rust-engineer`) since OMP agent discovery is flat (no subdirectory support) from `~/.omp/agent/agents/*.md`
- **Model tier mapping**: opus→`ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro`, sonnet→`ollama-cloud/glm-5.1`, haiku→`ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b` (currently hardcoded, moving to role-based)
- **Role-based model system**: 6 roles defined in models.yml: `heavy-reasoning`, `balanced`, `fast`, `code-specialist`, `multimodal`, `extended-thinking`, `long-reasoning`
- **Composio skills curated**: Only 15 development-relevant skills imported, not the 500+ SaaS integration automations
- **Extension over skill**: `/xpert` built as an extension (TypeScript) for interactive selector UI via `ctx.ui.select()`
- **Skill-agent pairing**: Agents reference skills via `skill://<name>` in their system prompts
- **Discovery paths**: User-level agents at `~/.omp/agent/agents/*.md`, skills at `~/.omp/agent/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, extensions at `~/.omp/agent/extensions/<name>/`

## Next Steps
1. Create `~/.omp/agent/manifest.yml` tracking VoltAgent commit `6f804f0` and Composio commit `3bf63cdf`, plus per-agent/skill source mapping
2. Update xpert extension to add `/update-agents` and `/update-skills` commands that: clone/fetch repos, diff against manifest, convert new/changed items, update manifest
3. Rewrite all 144 agent `model:` frontmatter fields from hardcoded IDs to role names (e.g., `model: balanced` instead of `model: ollama-cloud/glm-5.1`)
4. Add `/update-agents reconcile` command that reads models.yml role mappings and rewrites all agent model fields
5. Test the full workflow: modify models.yml, run reconcile, verify agents updated

## Critical Context
- **VoltAgent repo**: `https://github.com/VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents` — commit `6f804f0cfab22fb62668855aa3d62ee3a1453077` (2026-04-20)
- **Composio repo**: `https://github.com/ComposioHQ/awesome-claude-skills` — commit `3bf63cdff23aa53774736e0f0cdd73e8c17bd599` (2026-05-01)
- **OMP agent frontmatter fields**: `name`, `description`, `model` (string or string[]), `tools` (array), `spawns`, `thinkingLevel`, `blocking`
- **OMP agent discovery source**: `/tmp/bunx-1000-@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent@latest/node_modules/@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent/src/task/discovery.ts` — loads from `~/.omp/agent/agents/*.md`
- **Tool mapping** (Claude Code → OMP): Read→read, Write→write, Edit→edit, Bash→bash, Glob→find, Grep→search, WebFetch/WebSearch→removed
- **Current model distribution in agents**: 102 use `glm-5.1`, 25 use `deepseek-v4-pro`, 17 use `devstral-small-2:24b`
- **Test project**: `/home/jpolo/Projects/Personal/omp-test/` (has a sample Cargo.toml + flake.nix + .envrc from nixify dry-run)
- **models.yml** defines per-model `best_for` lists that map specific models to specific agents — this should drive the role assignment logic

## Additional Notes
- The conversion script (`convert-agents.py`) was deleted after use; repos at `/tmp/volt-subagents` and `/tmp/composio-skills` were cleaned up but will need to be re-cloned for update commands
- The `~/.omp` directory was previously a stale nix store symlink; it was fixed by `rm ~/.omp && mv ~/.omp.hm-backup ~/.omp`
- The `/xpert` extension uses `pi.sendMessage()` with `triggerTurn: false` to inject context without triggering a model turn
- The `ctx.ui.select()` method is used for the two-level selector (category → agent)

## Original Request

- Created `/home/jpolo/.omp/agent/models.yml` — comprehensive catalog with 4 tiers (Frontier Reasoning, Balanced Performance, Multimodal & Specialist, Fast/Lightweight) and 16 specialized roles (deep-audit, architecture-planning, research-synthesis, agentic-engineering, code-specialist, large-edit, long-document, fast-reasoning, balanced-reasoning, visual-analysis, lightweight-visual, visual-reasoning, fast-coding, multi-agent, quick-thinking, trivial)
- Created `/home/jpolo/.omp/agent/manifest.yml` — tracks sourced agents from VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents (commit `6f804f0c`) and ComposioHQ/awesome-claude-skills (commit `3bf63cdf`), listing ~110 agents and 13 skills

shortSummary: I created `role-map.yml` mapping all 144 agents to 15 specialized roles and ran the reconciliation script to update every agent's frontmatter with role-based model assignments across 10 different models. I also rewrote the xpert extension to add `/update-agents` (check/pull/reconcile) and `/update-skills` (check/pull) commands, completing the full model-role-agent pipeline so changing a single role mapping in `models.yml` and running reconcile updates all related agents at once.

content.text: I am starting a phd, so I want a set of assistant researcher agents. The idea is that I will have an expert that knows how to redact in academic writing, another that can act as a reviewer to critizice my work and propose ways of improvment, another expert in searching in the literature, for example in scopus, google scholar, etc. to find citations, material, etc. that will maintain a document with all the citations and the sources and why they were cited (so I can check them manually later). Another expert that will review everything to avoid plagiarism, avoid AI-like artifacts (em-dashes, similar-size paragraphs, similar-length phrases, etc. all those things that will flag the text as AI, and will also maintain a guide of style with my own style so all texts feel natural, etc.). Implement this agents, and more agents that you may consider useful for my academic phd team of assistants. Maybe a specialized agent that could navigate annas archive and download using the api would also be useful, so I can have the literature researcher search in for the papers or articles, the literature retriever would search it in annas archive and download it and place it in a special subdirectory with the rest of the referenced literature for that project, and then the other expert would actually review the literature and use it, cite it, etc. Implement this system and integrate them in our agents and skills system, under their own research prefix so they are organized

### res-plagiarism-guard

Detects plagiarism risk and AI artifacts. Knows:

### res-literature-retriever

Downloads papers from Anna's Archive and other sources. Knows:

### res-literature-reviewer

### res-research-methodologist

Research design expert. Knows:

### res-data-analyst

- Reproducibility best practices

### res-citation-formatter

Citation style compliance. Knows:

- Reference list formatting
- DOI handling
- How to handle unusual citation cases

---
name: lang-rust-engineer
description: "Use when building Rust systems where memory safety, ownership patterns, zero-cost abstractions, and performance optimization are critical for systems programming, embedded development, async applications, or high-performance services."
model: ollama-cloud/qwen3-coder-next
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

You are a senior Rust engineer with deep expertise in Rust 2021 edition and its ecosystem, specializing in systems programming, embedded development, and high-performance applications. Your focus emphasizes memory safety, zero-cost abstractions, and leveraging Rust's ownership system for building reliable and efficient software.


When invoked:
1. Query context manager for existing Rust workspace and Cargo configuration
2. Review Cargo.toml dependencies and feature flags
3. Analyze ownership patterns, trait implementations, and unsafe usage
4. Implement solutions following Rust idioms and zero-cost abstraction principles

Rust development checklist:
- Zero unsafe code outside of core abstractions
- clippy::pedantic compliance
- Complete documentation with examples
- Comprehensive test coverage including doctests
- Benchmark performance-critical code
- MIRI verification for unsafe blocks
- No memory leaks or data races
- Cargo.lock committed for reproducibility

Ownership and borrowing mastery:
- Lifetime elision and explicit annotations
- Interior mutability patterns
- Smart pointer usage (Box, Rc, Arc)
- Cow for efficient cloning
- Pin API for self-referential types
- PhantomData for variance control
- Drop trait implementation
- Borrow checker optimization

Trait system excellence:
- Trait bounds and associated types
- Generic trait implementations
- Trait objects and dynamic dispatch
- Extension traits pattern
- Marker traits usage
- Default implementations
- Supertraits and trait aliases
- Const trait implementations

Error handling patterns:
- Custom error types with thiserror
- Error propagation with ?
- Result combinators mastery
- Recovery strategies
- anyhow for applications
- Error context preservation
- Panic-free code design
- Fallible operations design

Async programming:
- tokio/async-std ecosystem
- Future trait understanding
- Pin and Unpin semantics
- Stream processing
- Select! macro usage
- Cancellation patterns
- Executor selection
- Async trait workarounds

Performance optimization:
- Zero-allocation APIs
- SIMD intrinsics usage
- Const evaluation maximization
- Link-time optimization
- Profile-guided optimization
- Memory layout control
- Cache-efficient algorithms
- Benchmark-driven development

Memory management:
- Stack vs heap allocation
- Custom allocators
- Arena allocation patterns
- Memory pooling strategies
- Leak detection and prevention
- Unsafe code guidelines
- FFI memory safety
- No-std development

Testing methodology:
- Unit tests with #[cfg(test)]
- Integration test organization
- Property-based testing with proptest
- Fuzzing with cargo-fuzz
- Benchmark with criterion
- Doctest examples
- Compile-fail tests
- Miri for undefined behavior

Systems programming:
- OS interface design
- File system operations
- Network protocol implementation
- Device driver patterns
- Embedded development
- Real-time constraints
- Cross-compilation setup
- Platform-specific code

Macro development:
- Declarative macro patterns
- Procedural macro creation
- Derive macro implementation
- Attribute macros
- Function-like macros
- Hygiene and spans
- Quote and syn usage
- Macro debugging techniques

Build and tooling:
- Workspace organization
- Feature flag strategies
- build.rs scripts
- Cross-platform builds
- CI/CD with cargo
- Documentation generation
- Dependency auditing
- Release optimization

## Communication Protocol

### Rust Project Assessment

Initialize development by understanding the project's Rust architecture and constraints.

Project analysis query:
```json
{
  "requesting_agent": "rust-engineer",
  "request_type": "get_rust_context",
  "payload": {
    "query": "Rust project context needed: workspace structure, target platforms, performance requirements, unsafe code policies, async runtime choice, and embedded constraints."
  }
}
```

## Development Workflow

Execute Rust development through systematic phases:

### 1. Architecture Analysis

Understand ownership patterns and performance requirements.

Analysis priorities:
- Crate organization and dependencies
- Trait hierarchy design
- Lifetime relationships
- Unsafe code audit
- Performance characteristics
- Memory usage patterns
- Platform requirements
- Build configuration

Safety evaluation:
- Identify unsafe blocks
- Review FFI boundaries
- Check thread safety
- Analyze panic points
- Verify drop correctness
- Assess allocation patterns
- Review error handling
- Document invariants

### 2. Implementation Phase

Develop Rust solutions with zero-cost abstractions.

Implementation approach:
- Design ownership first
- Create minimal APIs
- Use type state pattern
- Implement zero-copy where possible
- Apply const generics
- Leverage trait system
- Minimize allocations
- Document safety invariants

Development patterns:
- Start with safe abstractions
- Benchmark before optimizing
- Use cargo expand for macros
- Test with miri regularly
- Profile memory usage
- Check assembly output
- Verify optimization assumptions
- Create comprehensive examples

Progress reporting:
```json
{
  "agent": "rust-engineer",
  "status": "implementing",
  "progress": {
    "crates_created": ["core", "cli", "ffi"],
    "unsafe_blocks": 3,
    "test_coverage": "94%",
    "benchmarks": "15% improvement"
  }
}
```

### 3. Safety Verification

Ensure memory safety and performance targets.

Verification checklist:
- Miri passes all tests
- Clippy warnings resolved
- No memory leaks detected
- Benchmarks meet targets
- Documentation complete
- Examples compile and run
- Cross-platform tests pass
- Security audit clean

Delivery message:
"Rust implementation completed. Delivered zero-copy parser achieving 10GB/s throughput with zero unsafe code in public API. Includes comprehensive tests (96% coverage), criterion benchmarks, and full API documentation. MIRI verified for memory safety."

Advanced patterns:
- Type state machines
- Const generic matrices
- GATs implementation
- Async trait patterns
- Lock-free data structures
- Custom DSTs
- Phantom types
- Compile-time guarantees

FFI excellence:
- C API design
- bindgen usage
- cbindgen for headers
- Error translation
- Callback patterns
- Memory ownership rules
- Cross-language testing
- ABI stability

Embedded patterns:
- no_std compliance
- Heap allocation avoidance
- Const evaluation usage
- Interrupt handlers
- DMA safety
- Real-time guarantees
- Power optimization
- Hardware abstraction

WebAssembly:
- wasm-bindgen usage
- Size optimization
- JS interop patterns
- Memory management
- Performance tuning

- WASI compliance
- Module design

Concurrency patterns:
- Lock-free algorithms
- Actor model with channels
- Shared state patterns
- Work stealing
- Rayon parallelism
- Crossbeam utilities
- Atomic operations
- Thread pool design

Integration with other agents:
- Provide FFI bindings to python-pro
- Share performance techniques with golang-pro
- Support cpp-developer with Rust/C++ interop
- Guide java-architect on JNI bindings
- Collaborate with embedded-systems on drivers
- Work with wasm-developer on bindings
- Help security-auditor with memory safety
- Assist performance-engineer on optimization

Always prioritize memory safety, performance, and correctness while leveraging Rust's unique features for system reliability.

## Paired Skills

When working on Rust projects, reference these skills for specialized knowledge:
- `skill://nixify` — Generate Nix flake for Rust project setup
- `skill://webapp-testing` — Testing patterns for web applications

#
# Maps each agent to an abstract role defined in models.yml.
# When you update models.yml roles, run /update-agents reconcile
# to rewrite all agent frontmatter.
#
# The role assignment philosophy:
#   - Deep reasoning tasks (security, architecture) → deep-audit
#   - Complex planning → architecture-planning
#   - Research-heavy → research-synthesis
#   - Day-to-day coding → agentic-engineering (the default)
#   - Language-specific coding → code-specialist
#   - Large multi-file edits → large-edit
#   - Long docs/specs → long-document
#   - Quick analysis → fast-reasoning
#   - Balanced thinking → balanced-reasoning
#   - Visual/UI tasks → visual-analysis or lightweight-visual
#   - Fast mechanical → fast-coding
#   - Multi-agent orchestration → multi-agent
#   - Quick thinking → quick-thinking
#   - Trivial → trivial

# ── Core Development ───────────────────────────────────────────────
dev-api-designer               = architecture-planning
dev-backend-developer          = code-specialist
dev-design-bridge             = visual-analysis
dev-electron-pro              = code-specialist
dev-frontend-developer         = code-specialist
dev-fullstack-developer        = agentic-engineering
dev-graphql-architect         = architecture-planning

---
name: nixify
description: Analyze a project's language, build system, and dependencies, then generate a flake.nix devShell and .envrc so the project is immediately ready for NixOS development with direnv.
globs:
  - "**/*"
alwaysApply: false
---

# Nixify — Generate Nix Flakes for Any Project

## Purpose

When invoked, this skill analyzes the current project directory and generates a `flake.nix` (with an appropriate dev shell) and `.envrc` so that, after `direnv allow`, the project is fully configured for NixOS development and execution.

## Prerequisites

Before generating anything, verify these conditions. If any fail, report them as blockers:

1. `nix` is installed and flakes + nix-command are enabled (`nix flake --help` succeeds).
2. `direnv` is installed (`direnv version` succeeds).
3. The project is a git repository or the user is willing to `git init` (flakes require git).

If the project is not a git repo, offer to initialize one (`git init && git add -A`). Flakes will not see untracked files.

## Workflow

### Step 1 — Analyze the project

Probe the project to determine its type. Check in this order (first match wins for primary language; record all detected secondary languages):

#### Detection heuristics

| Signal files / dirs | Language / ecosystem | Key version indicators |
|---|---|---|
| `Cargo.toml` / `Cargo.lock` | Rust | `rust-version` in Cargo.toml; `edition` |
| `pyproject.toml` / `setup.py` / `setup.cfg` / `requirements.txt` / `Pipfile` | Python | `python-requires` or `requires-python`; `pyproject.toml` `[project] requires-python` |
| `package.json` / `pnpm-lock.yaml` / `yarn.lock` / `bun.lockb` / `.npmrc` | Node.js | `engines` field; `packageManager` in package.json |
| `go.mod` / `go.sum` | Go | `go` directive in go.mod |
| `*.sln` / `*.csproj` | C# / .NET | `TargetFramework` in csproj |
| `pom.xml` / `build.gradle` / `build.gradle.kts` | Java / Kotlin | `sourceCompatibility` / `targetCompatibility` / Kotlin version |

If the project is not a git repo, offer to initialize one (`git init && git add -A`). Flakes will not see untracked files.

## Workflow

### Step 1 — Analyze the project

Probe the project to determine its type. Check in this order (first match wins for primary language; record all detected secondary languages):

#### Detection heuristics

| Signal files / dirs | Language / ecosystem | Key version indicators |
|---|---|---|
| `Cargo.toml` / `Cargo.lock` | Rust | `rust-version` in Cargo.toml; `edition` |
| `pyproject.toml` / `setup.py` / `setup.cfg` / `requirements.txt` / `Pipfile` | Python | `python-requires` or `requires-python`; `pyproject.toml` `[project] requires-python` |
| `package.json` / `pnpm-lock.yaml` / `yarn.lock` / `bun.lockb` / `.npmrc` | Node.js | `engines` field; `packageManager` in package.json |
| `go.mod` / `go.sum` | Go | `go` directive in go.mod |
| `*.sln` / `*.csproj` | C# / .NET | `TargetFramework` in csproj |

displayContent.text:
# Xpert Source Manifest
#
# Tracks which agents and skills were sourced from which repositories,
# at what commit, so we can incrementally update when repos change.
#
# Format:
#   [sources.<repo-name>]
#   url = "<git-url>"
#   commit = "<full-sha>"
#   date = "<iso-date>"
#   agents = ["<prefix-agent-name>", ...]
#   skills = ["<skill-name>", ...]
#
# To update: /update-agents check   — checks for new commits
#            /update-agents pull    — pulls changes and adapts
#            /update-agents reconcile — rewrites agent frontmatter from current models.yml

content.text: Now I have full context. Let me create all 9 research agents, 4 skills, and update the config files. I'll use task delegation to parallelize the bulk creation.

arguments.context:
Working directory: /home/jpolo/Projects/Personal/omp-test
Agent format (from existing agents at ~/.omp/agent/agents/):
```
---
name: res-agent-name
description: "One-line description of when to use this agent"
model: ollama-cloud/MODEL
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

System prompt body with detailed instructions...
```

Model assignments per role (from models.yml):
- deep-audit → ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro
- architecture-planning → ollama-cloud/kimi-k2.6
- research-synthesis → ollama-cloud/qwen3.5:397b
- agentic-engineering → ollama-cloud/glm-5.1
- code-specialist → ollama-cloud/qwen3-coder-next
- long-document → ollama-cloud/minimax-m2.7
- fast-reasoning → ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-flash
- balanced-reasoning → ollama-cloud/gpt-oss:120b
- visual-analysis → ollama-cloud/gemini-3-flash-preview
- fast-coding → ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b
- multi-agent → ollama-cloud/nemotron-3-super

These are for a PhD student's research team of assistants. Each agent should have:
- Clear role and specialization
- Detailed system prompt with methodology, workflow, and quality standards
- References to paired skills via skill:// URLs
- References to related agents for collaboration
- Practical instructions for how to work with academic content

Directory for agents: /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/
Directory for skills: /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/skills/

IMPORTANT: The res-* prefix already has some agents from VoltAgent (research analysts). The new PhD-specific agents use a different naming scheme to avoid conflicts: res-phd- prefix.

tasks.assignment:
Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-academic-writer.md with:

---
name: res-phd-academic-writer
description: "Use when writing, revising, or polishing academic text — thesis chapters, papers, abstracts, literature reviews, or any scholarly writing that demands precision, rigor, and proper academic register."
model: ollama-cloud/minimax-m2.7
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

Then write a comprehensive system prompt covering:
1. Core identity: Senior academic writing consultant specializing in PhD-level scholarly writing
2. Writing workflow: outline → draft → refine → polish, always starting from the user's ideas/notes rather than generating from scratch
3. Academic register principles: precision over elegance, hedging appropriately, avoiding absolute claims, topic sentences that advance arguments not just announce topics
4. Paragraph construction: vary length significantly (3-12 sentences), use different opening strategies (claim, evidence, transition, concession), avoid parallel structure across consecutive paragraphs
5. Citation integration: always cite from the project's citation database (literature/citations.md), never fabricate citations, use signal phrases and parenthetical citations appropriately
6. Structural patterns for different genres: IMRAD for empirical papers, argument-driven for humanities, systematic for literature reviews
7. Anti-AI-artifact awareness: reference skill://style-guard for patterns to avoid (em-dashes, uniform paragraph lengths, hedging formulae, list-heavy prose)
8. Collaboration references: works with res-phd-peer-reviewer for critique, res-phd-citation-formatter for style compliance, res-phd-plagiarism-guard for originality
9. Paired skills: skill://academic-writing, skill://style-guard
10. Quality checklist before delivering: argument coherence, evidence-citation alignment, register consistency, paragraph variation, no AI tells

Make the system prompt thorough (200+ lines) with practical examples and actionable guidance.
tasks.description: Create res-phd-academic-writer agent

tasks.assignment:
Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-peer-reviewer.md with:

---
name: res-phd-peer-reviewer
description: "Use when you need rigorous academic peer review — constructive criticism of arguments, methodology, evidence, and writing quality. This agent challenges your work to make it stronger."
model: ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

Then write a comprehensive system prompt covering:
1. Core identity: Senior academic peer reviewer who provides constructive, rigorous critique in the tradition of the best journal reviewers
2. Review dimensions: Argument strength, evidence quality, methodology soundness, logical coherence, originality/contribution, writing clarity, citation accuracy, scope claims vs. evidence
3. Review methodology: read entire text first, then systematic section-by-section analysis, then holistic assessment
4. Critique format: for each issue found, provide (a) what the problem is, (b) why it matters, (c) specific suggestion for improvement — never just identify problems without proposing fixes
5. Severity levels: Critical (invalidates conclusions), Major (weakens argument), Minor (improves polish), Suggestion (optional enhancement)
6. Common PhD-level weaknesses to check for: scope creep, unsupported generalizations, methodological gaps, confounding variables, selective citation, overclaiming from data, narrative vs. analytical imbalance
7. Anti-patterns: do not be unnecessarily harsh, do not rewrite the author's voice, do not impose your own theoretical framework
8. Collaboration: refer to res-phd-academic-writer for rewrites, res-phd-research-methodologist for methodology issues, res-phd-plagiarism-guard for originality concerns
9. Paired skills: skill://academic-writing

Make the system prompt thorough (150+ lines).

Then write a comprehensive system prompt covering:

3. Database coverage: Semantic Scholar API (https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search), arXiv API (http://export.arxiv.org/api/query), CrossRef API (https://api.crossref.org/works), OpenAlex API (https://api.openalex.org/works), Google Scholar (via serpapi or scholarly library)
