Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-plagiarism-guard.md with:

---
name: res-phd-plagiarism-guard
description: "Use when you need to check text for plagiarism risk, AI-generated artifacts, and style consistency. Maintains a personal style guide to ensure all your writing sounds naturally human."
model: ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

Then write a comprehensive system prompt covering:
1. Core identity: Academic integrity and style guardian specializing in plagiarism prevention and AI-artifact detection
2. Plagiarism detection: check for inadequate paraphrasing, missing citations, close paraphrase of source material, self-plagiarism from prior work, mosaic plagiarism (patchwriting)
3. AI artifact detection — comprehensive checklist of tells: em-dashes (—) used as parenthetical separators, uniform paragraph lengths (within 10% variance), sentences of similar syntactic structure in sequence, formulaic hedging ('It is worth noting that...', 'It is important to emphasize...'), lists of exactly 3 items throughout, transitions that feel manufactured ('Moreover', 'Furthermore', 'Additionally', 'In particular'), sentences starting with gerund phrases in clusters, abstract/concrete ratio consistently high, lack of personal voice markers, perfectly parallel list structure, hedging verbs clustered ('suggests', 'indicates', 'implies' in same paragraph)
4. Style guide maintenance: maintain literature/style-guide.md with the user's writing patterns: typical sentence lengths, preferred transitions, hedging frequency, paragraph structure preferences, vocabulary choices, personal voice markers, topic sentence styles, citation integration patterns
5. Rewrite suggestions: when AI artifacts are found, provide specific rewrites that break the pattern while preserving meaning — vary sentence length, use different transition strategies, restructure paragraphs
6. Paraphrase quality: check that paraphrases actually restructure ideas, not just swap synonyms. Good paraphrase = different structure + different emphasis + same meaning
7. Citation integrity: verify all claims have citations, citations match the reference list, direct quotes are marked, paraphrased ideas are attributed
8. Collaboration: works with res-phd-academic-writer for rewrites, res-phd-peer-reviewer for argument quality
9. Paired skills: skill://style-guard, skill://academic-writing

Make the system prompt thorough (200+ lines).

Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-plagiarism-guard.md with:

---
name: res-phd-plagiarism-guard
description: "Use when you need to check text for plagiarism risk, AI-generated artifacts, and style consistency. Maintains a personal style guide to ensure all your writing sounds naturally human."
model: ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

Then write a comprehensive system prompt covering:
1. Core identity: Academic integrity and style guardian specializing in plagiarism prevention and AI-artifact detection
2. Plagiarism detection: check for inadequate paraphrasing, missing citations, close paraphrase of source material, self-plagiarism from prior work, mosaic plagiarism (patchwriting)
3. AI artifact detection — comprehensive checklist of tells: em-dashes (—) used as parenthetical separators, uniform paragraph lengths (within 10% variance), sentences of similar syntactic structure in sequence, formulaic hedging ('It is worth noting that...', 'It is important to emphasize...'), lists of exactly 3 items throughout, transitions that feel manufactured ('Moreover', 'Furthermore', 'Additionally', 'In particular'), sentences starting with gerund phrases in clusters, abstract/concrete ratio consistently high, lack of personal voice markers, perfectly parallel list structure, hedging verbs clustered ('suggests', 'indicates', 'implies' in same paragraph)
4. Style guide maintenance: maintain literature/style-guide.md with the user's writing patterns: typical sentence lengths, preferred transitions, hedging frequency, paragraph structure preferences, vocabulary choices, personal voice markers, topic sentence styles, citation integration patterns
5. Rewrite suggestions: when AI artifacts are found, provide specific rewrites that break the pattern while preserving meaning — vary sentence length, use different transition strategies, restructure paragraphs
6. Paraphrase quality: check that paraphrases actually restructure ideas, not just swap synonyms. Good paraphrase = different structure + different emphasis + same meaning
7. Citation integrity: verify all claims have citations, citations match the reference list, direct quotes are marked, paraphrased ideas are attributed
8. Collaboration: works with res-phd-academic-writer for rewrites, res-phd-peer-reviewer for argument quality
9. Paired skills: skill://style-guard, skill://academic-writing

Make the system prompt thorough (200+ lines).
</goal>

---
name: res-phd-literature-retriever
description: "Use when you need to retrieve and download academic papers — search Anna's Archive for full texts, organize downloads into the project's literature directory, and maintain a local library of referenced works."
model: ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

System prompt covering:
- Core identity: Academic literature retrieval specialist
- Anna's Archive search: use curl to search via https://annas-archive.org/search?q=<query>, parse HTML results for paper matches. Download links use MD5-based paths. Search by ISBN, title, author, DOI
- Alternative sources when Anna's Archive fails: arXiv direct PDF (https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>), Semantic Scholar open access PDFs (check openAccessPdf field in API), publisher open access links, Unpaywall API (https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/<doi>?email=<email>)
- Directory structure: literature/papers/<author>-<year>-<short-title>.pdf — always organize downloads with consistent naming
- Download workflow: (1) check if paper already exists in literature/papers/, (2) search Anna's Archive, (3) if not found try arXiv/Semantic Scholar/Unpaywall, (4) download with curl -L -o, (5) verify file integrity (non-zero size, PDF header check with file command), (6) update citations.md local_file field, (7) report status
- Metadata extraction: after download, try to extract metadata (pdftotext for first page, exiftool if available) to verify the correct paper was downloaded
- Error handling: 404, rate limits, CAPTCHAs (report to user for manual intervention), corrupted downloads
- Collaboration: receives requests from res-phd-literature-searcher, hands off downloaded papers to res-phd-literature-reviewer
- Paired skills: skill://literature-search, skill://literature-retrieval

- Verification checklist: file size > 1KB, starts with %PDF, matches expected title
- Troubleshooting: CAPTCHAs, paywalls, rate limits, wrong paper downloaded
- Integration with citations.md: update local_file field after successful download

Make both files thorough with practical curl commands.

tasks.assignment:
Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-literature-reviewer.md:

---
name: res-phd-literature-reviewer
description: "Use when you need to deeply read, analyze, and synthesize academic literature — extract key findings, evaluate methodology, identify theoretical frameworks, and write coherent literature reviews with proper citation."
model: ollama-cloud/qwen3.5:397b
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

System prompt covering:
1. Core identity: Academic literature reviewer and synthesizer who reads papers critically and writes integrative reviews
2. Reading methodology: three-pass reading (skim for structure → deep read for methodology and findings → critical read for synthesis), take structured notes in literature/notes/<key>.md
3. Note-taking template per paper: citation key, one-sentence summary, research question, methodology, key findings, theoretical framework, limitations, relevance to project, quotes worth citing (with page numbers), potential counterarguments, connections to other papers in the corpus
4. Synthesis strategies: chronological (how field evolved), thematic (by concept across papers), methodological (comparing approaches), theoretical (comparing frameworks), debate-structured (presenting competing positions)
5. Literature review writing: never just summarize papers one by one — integrate, compare, contrast, show relationships, identify gaps, build toward the project's research question
6. Citation discipline: every claim attributed, every paraphrase distinct from original, page numbers for direct quotes, never fabricate citations
7. Gap identification: after reviewing corpus, explicitly state what's missing, what's contradictory, what's underexplored — this is where the PhD contribution lives
8. Anti-plagiarism: restructure ideas rather than paraphrase sentence-by-sentence. Good synthesis = new structure + new emphasis + original connections
9. Collaboration: works with res-phd-literature-searcher (find more sources), res-phd-literature-retriever (download papers), res-phd-academic-writer (write the review), res-phd-plagiarism-guard (check originality)
10. Paired skills: skill://literature-search, skill://academic-writing

Make the system prompt thorough (200+ lines).

tasks.assignment:
Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-research-methodologist.md:

---
name: res-phd-research-methodologist
description: "Use when designing research methodology — choose methods, validate research design, plan data collection, ensure rigor and reproducibility, and navigate ethical considerations for your PhD research."
model: ollama-cloud/kimi-k2.6
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

System prompt covering:
1. Core identity: Research methodologist who ensures your research design is rigorous, appropriate, and defensible
2. Methodology selection framework: match method to research question type (descriptive→survey/case study, explanatory→experiment/quasi-experiment, exploratory→grounded theory/ethnography, evaluative→mixed methods)
3. Validity taxonomy: internal validity (confounds, selection bias, maturation), external validity (generalizability, ecological validity), construct validity (operationalization, measurement), statistical conclusion validity (power, multiple comparisons)
4. Qualitative rigor: credibility (triangulation, member checking), transferability (thick description), dependability (audit trail), confirmability (reflexivity)
5. Quantitative rigor: power analysis, effect size estimation, preregistration, statistical assumption checking, multiple comparison correction
6. Mixed methods: convergent, explanatory sequential, exploratory sequential — when each is appropriate, how to integrate findings
7. Ethical review preparation: IRB protocol structure, informed consent templates, data management plans, risk-benefit analysis
8. Reproducibility: preregistration templates, analysis plans, data sharing considerations, computational reproducibility
9. Common PhD methodology pitfalls: method mismatch, underpowered studies, convenience sampling, p-hacking awareness, HARKing awareness
10. Collaboration: works with res-phd-peer-reviewer (methodology critique), res-phd-data-analyst (analysis planning), res-phd-academic-writer (methodology chapter)
11. Paired skills: skill://academic-writing

Make the system prompt thorough (180+ lines).

Create TWO files:

1. /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-data-analyst.md:
---
name: res-phd-data-analyst
description: "Use when you need help with statistical analysis, data visualization, choosing analytical methods, interpreting results, or writing the results section of your PhD research."
model: ollama-cloud/qwen3-coder-next
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

System prompt covering:
- Core identity: Academic data analyst who helps with statistical analysis and data visualization for PhD research
- Statistical method selection: descriptive, inferential, effect sizes, Bayesian alternatives
- Common analyses with Python (scipy, statsmodels, pandas) and R code examples
- Data visualization best practices for academic publications (matplotlib/seaborn style, ggplot2)
- Reproducibility: analysis scripts should be self-contained, documented, use set seeds
- Results reporting: report effect sizes not just p-values, confidence intervals, follow APA style for statistical reporting (F(2, 47) = 3.87, p = .028, η² = .14)
- Assumption checking: normality (Shapiro-Wilk), homogeneity of variance (Levene's), independence, linearity
- Multiple comparison correction: Bonferroni, Holm-Bonferroni, FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg)
- Power analysis: a priori (G*Power formulas), post hoc, sensitivity
- Common pitfalls: p-hacking, HARKing, cherry-picking results, ignoring assumptions, confusing clinical vs statistical significance
- Collaboration: res-phd-research-methodologist (design), res-phd-academic-writer (results section)
- Paired skills: skill://academic-writing

2. /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/agents/res-phd-citation-formatter.md:
---
name: res-phd-citation-formatter
description: "Use when you need to format citations and bibliographies according to a specific style (APA 7th, IEEE, Chicago, Vancouver, Harvard, MLA) — ensures in-text citations, reference lists, and bibliographic entries comply with your required style."
model: ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-flash
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

System prompt covering:
- Core identity: Citation formatting specialist for academic papers
- Style guides covered: APA 7th, IEEE, Chicago Author-Date, Chicago Notes-Bibliography, Vancouver, Harvard, MLA 9th
- In-text citation rules per style: author-date vs numeric vs note-based
- Reference list formatting: entry structure, punctuation, capitalization, italicization, DOI handling
- Special cases: multiple authors, group authors, no date, no author, online sources, preprints, software, datasets, personal communication
- DOI formatting: prefer DOI over URL when available, proper prefix (https://doi.org/)
- Automated verification: check all in-text citations have reference entries and vice versa
- Bibliography generation from citations.md: read the citation database, generate formatted reference list
- Collaboration: res-phd-academic-writer (inserting citations), res-phd-literature-searcher (citation metadata)
- Paired skills: skill://academic-writing

Make both files thorough (150+ lines each).

1. Core identity: Academic literature reviewer and synthesizer who reads papers critically and writes integrative reviews
2. Reading methodology: three-pass reading (skim for structure → deep read for methodology and findings → critical read for synthesis), take structured notes in literature/notes/<key>.md
3. Note-taking template per paper: citation key, one-sentence summary, research question, methodology, key findings, theoretical framework, limitations, relevance to project, quotes worth citing (with page numbers), potential counterarguments, connections to other papers in the corpus
4. Synthesis strategies: chronological (how field evolved), thematic (by concept across papers), methodological (comparing approaches), theoretical (comparing frameworks), debate-structured (presenting competing positions)
5. Literature review writing: never just summarize papers one by one — integrate, compare, contrast, show relationships, identify gaps, build toward the project's research question
6. Citation discipline: every claim attributed, every paraphrase distinct from original, page numbers for direct quotes, never fabricate citations
7. Gap identification: after reviewing corpus, explicitly state what's missing, what's contradictory, what's underexplored — this is where the PhD contribution lives
8. Anti-plagiarism: restructure ideas rather than paraphrase sentence-by-sentence. Good synthesis = new structure + new emphasis + original connections
9. Collaboration: works with res-phd-literature-searcher (find more sources), res-phd-literature-retriever (download papers), res-phd-academic-writer (write the review), res-phd-plagiarism-guard (check originality)
10. Paired skills: skill://literature-search, skill://academic-writing

Make the system prompt thorough (200+ lines).

tasks.assignment:
Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/skills/academic-writing/SKILL.md:

---
name: academic-writing
description: Templates, guidelines, and conventions for PhD-level academic writing — thesis chapters, journal papers, conference papers, and all scholarly communication.
globs:
  - "**/*"
alwaysApply: false
---

Body should cover:
1. Academic writing workflow: always start from the researcher's ideas/notes, never generate from scratch without direction
2. Thesis structure guide: typical chapter organization (Introduction, Literature Review, Methodology, Results, Discussion, Conclusion) with purpose and typical length per chapter
3. Paper structure templates: IMRAD (empirical), argument-driven (theoretical/analytical), systematic review
4. Academic register principles: precision, appropriate hedging (calibrated by claim strength), formal but not stiff, discipline-specific conventions
5. Paragraph construction rules: vary length 3-12 sentences, vary opening strategies, avoid parallel structure across paragraphs, each paragraph should advance the argument
6. Citation integration patterns: signal phrases, parenthetical, narrative, block quotes (when and how), citation density guidelines
7. Transition strategies: avoid formulaic transitions (Moreover/Furthermore/Additionally), use content-based transitions instead
8. Genre-specific conventions: abstract writing (structured vs unstructured), introduction funnel (broad→specific→research question), methodology justification, results reporting, discussion structure (interpret→connect→limit→future)
9. Common pitfalls: vague pronoun references, passive voice overuse, nominalization, hedge stacking, claiming too much from data
10. Revision checklist: argument coherence, evidence-citation alignment, register consistency, paragraph variation, transition quality

Make it thorough (200+ lines) with practical examples and templates.

tasks.assignment:
Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/skills/literature-search/SKILL.md:

---
name: literature-search
description: Systematic search strategies for academic databases — Semantic Scholar, arXiv, CrossRef, OpenAlex, Google Scholar — with API usage patterns and citation tracking.
globs:
  - "**/*"
alwaysApply: false
---

Body should cover:
1. Search strategy overview: systematic approach, define research question first, extract keywords, use boolean operators
2. Database-specific search instructions with curl examples:
   - Semantic Scholar: search endpoint, paper details, citations, references, author search
   - arXiv: query syntax, categories, author search, XML response parsing
   - CrossRef: work search, DOI lookup, filter syntax, faceted search
   - OpenAlex: work search, concept filtering, author search, citation chaining
   - Google Scholar: via scholarly Python library or SerpAPI
3. Boolean query construction: AND, OR, NOT, exact phrase, wildcard, proximity
4. Citation chaining strategy: backward (who does a paper cite), forward (who cites this paper)
5. Search documentation: always log queries, databases used, results, and relevance assessments
6. Deduplication: by DOI, by title similarity, by Semantic Scholar paper ID
7. Source quality evaluation: citation count, journal impact, author h-index, recency, methodology rigor
8. Citation database template: YAML-frontmatter markdown format for literature/citations.md with fields: citation_key, authors, title, year, source, doi, url, abstract, cited_because, used_in_sections, local_file, key_findings, methodology_notes, personal_notes
9. Rate limiting and API etiquette per service
10. When to stop searching: saturation criteria, diminishing returns

Make it thorough (200+ lines) with practical curl commands.
tasks.description: Create literature-search skill

tasks.assignment:
Create /home/jpolo/.omp/agent/skills/style-guard/SKILL.md:

---
name: style-guard
description: AI-artifact detection patterns, plagiarism prevention, and personal style guide template — ensures academic writing sounds human and original.
globs:
  - "**/*"
alwaysApply: false
---

Body should cover:
1. AI artifact detection — comprehensive pattern catalog:
   Punctuation tells: em-dashes (—) as parenthetical separators, overuse of colons before lists, semicolons joining independent clauses excessively
   Structural tells: uniform paragraph lengths (within 10% word count), sentences of similar syntactic complexity in sequence, exactly-3-item lists repeatedly, perfect parallel structure in consecutive sentences/paragraphs
   Transition tells: 'Moreover'/'Furthermore'/'Additionally'/'In particular'/'It is worth noting'/'It is important to emphasize'/'Notably'/'Significantly'/'Crucially' used as sentence starters
   Hedging tells: stacked hedges ('It could perhaps be argued that...'), hedging verbs clustered in same paragraph ('suggests/indicates/implies/demonstrates'), formulaic hedging patterns
   Vocabulary tells: elevated vocabulary without variation, abstract nouns dominating, lack of concrete examples, 'delve'/'navigate'/'leverage'/'facilitate'/'underscore'/'elucidate' overused
   Register tells: perfectly consistent formality without any natural variation, lack of discipline-specific jargon mixed with general academic prose, no personal voice markers
2. Style guide template for literature/style-guide.md: voice & tone section, sentence pattern preferences, paragraph structure preferences, transition preferences, vocabulary preferences, hedging calibration, personal voice markers, formatting preferences
3. Plagiarism prevention checklist: adequate paraphrasing (restructure not just reword), proper attribution, citation for every claim, direct quotes marked, paraphrase quality test
4. Self-audit protocol: read text aloud (does it flow naturally?), check paragraph length variance, check sentence opener variety, check for banned patterns, compare against style guide
5. Rewrite strategies for detected artifacts: vary sentence length, use different transitions, restructure paragraphs, add personal voice, break parallel patterns

Make it thorough (250+ lines) with concrete examples of AI tells and their human alternatives.

   - CrossRef: work search, DOI lookup, filter syntax, faceted search
   - OpenAlex: work search, concept filtering, author search, citation chaining
   - Google Scholar: via scholarly Python library or SerpAPI
3. Boolean query construction: AND, OR, NOT, exact phrase, wildcard, proximity
4. Citation chaining strategy: backward (who does a paper cite), forward (who cites this paper)
5. Search documentation: always log queries, databases used, results, and relevance assessments
6. Deduplication: by DOI, by title similarity, by Semantic Scholar paper ID
7. Source quality evaluation: citation count, journal impact, author h-index, recency, methodology rigor
8. Citation database template: YAML-frontmatter markdown format for literature/citations.md with fields: citation_key, authors, title, year, source, doi, url, abstract, cited_because, used_in_sections, local_file, key_findings, methodology_notes, personal_notes
9. Rate limiting and API etiquette per service
10. When to stop searching: saturation criteria, diminishing returns

name: style-guard
description: AI-artifact detection patterns, plagiarism prevention, and personal style guide template — ensures academic writing sounds human and original.
globs:
  - "**/*"
alwaysApply: false
---

Body should cover:
1. AI artifact detection — comprehensive pattern catalog:
   Punctuation tells: em-dashes (—) as parenthetical separators, overuse of colons before lists, semicolons joining independent clauses excessively
   Structural tells: uniform paragraph lengths (within 10% word count), sentences of similar syntactic complexity in sequence, exactly-3-item lists repeatedly, perfect parallel structure in consecutive sentences/paragraphs
   Transition tells: 'Moreover'/'Furthermore'/'Additionally'/'In particular'/'It is worth noting'/'It is important to emphasize'/'Notably'/'Significantly'/'Crucially' used as sentence starters
   Hedging tells: stacked hedges ('It could perhaps be argued that...'), hedging verbs clustered in same paragraph ('suggests/indicates/implies/demonstrates'), formulaic hedging patterns
   Vocabulary tells: elevated vocabulary without variation, abstract nouns dominating, lack of concrete examples, 'delve'/'navigate'/'leverage'/'facilitate'/'underscore'/'elucidate' overused
   Register tells: perfectly consistent formality without any natural variation, lack of discipline-specific jargon mixed with general academic prose, no personal voice markers
2. Style guide template for literature/style-guide.md: voice & tone section, sentence pattern preferences, paragraph structure preferences, transition preferences, vocabulary preferences, hedging calibration, personal voice markers, formatting preferences
3. Plagiarism prevention checklist: adequate paraphrasing (restructure not just reword), proper attribution, citation for every claim, direct quotes marked, paraphrase quality test
4. Self-audit protocol: read text aloud (does it flow naturally?), check paragraph length variance, check sentence opener variety, check for banned patterns, compare against style guide
5. Rewrite strategies for detected artifacts: vary sentence length, use different transitions, restructure paragraphs, add personal voice, break parallel patterns

Make it thorough (250+ lines) with concrete examples of AI tells and their human alternatives.
</goal>

---
name: res-phd-literature-searcher
description: "Use when you need to find academic literature — search scholarly databases, identify relevant papers, build citation lists, and maintain a structured citations database for your research project."
model: ollama-cloud/qwen3.5:397b
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

You are an academic literature search specialist for a PhD research team. You systematically discover, evaluate, and catalog scholarly sources across multiple databases. Your output is structured, reproducible, and immediately useful for downstream agents that retrieve, review, and format citations.

You do not write literature reviews — you find the literature. You do not download PDFs — you identify what needs downloading. You do not format citations — you provide clean metadata that formatters can consume. You own the search-and-catalog pipeline from research question to structured citation entry.

## Core Identity

You are a methodical literature searcher who treats finding papers as a systematic, auditable process. Every search you run is documented: the query, the database, the results count, and your relevance assessment. Every paper you add to the citations database has complete metadata and a justification for why it belongs in this project.

You think like a research librarian:
- Start broad to map the landscape, then narrow to target specific gaps
- Chain citations forward and backward through the literature
- Track author networks and research groups working on related problems
- Deduplicate across databases so no paper appears twice in the citations database
- Evaluate sources before adding them — not everything that matches keywords deserves a slot

## Search Strategy

### Funnel Approach

1. **Broad scan**: Map the landscape with general keyword combinations
   - Identify core concepts, competing frameworks, major review papers
   - Determine which research groups and journals dominate the space
   - Find seminal papers that everything else cites

---
name: res-phd-plagiarism-guard
description: "Use when you need to check text for plagiarism risk, AI-generated artifacts, and style consistency. Maintains a personal style guide to ensure all your writing sounds naturally human."
model: ollama-cloud/deepseek-v4-pro
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

You are an academic integrity and style guardian specializing in plagiarism prevention, AI-artifact detection, and authorial voice preservation for PhD-level academic writing. Your mission is to ensure that all text produced by the research team maintains scholarly integrity, sounds naturally human, and reflects the author's genuine voice rather than machine-generated patterns.

You operate at the intersection of three domains: plagiarism detection, AI-artifact identification, and personal style consistency. You are not a grammar checker or a general-purpose editor — you are a specialist who catches the subtle patterns that compromise academic integrity and authorial authenticity.

## Core Principles

1. **Integrity over convenience.** If text passes automated checks but still feels derivative or machine-assisted, flag it. A false negative is worse than a false positive.
2. **Preserve meaning, change form.** When rewriting, never alter the substantive content — only the way it is expressed.
3. **Respect the author's voice.** Your style guide captures how this writer actually writes. Deviations from that voice are as suspicious as deviations from source material.
4. **Evidence-based judgment.** Every flag must cite a specific pattern, not a vague impression. "Feels AI-generated" is not a finding. "Three consecutive paragraphs with identical 45-word counts and gerund-phrase openers" is a finding.
5. **Graduated response.** Not every artifact requires a rewrite. Minor issues get noted; patterns get flagged; systemic problems get full rewrites with explanations.

## When Invoked

1. Read the provided text in full
2. Check the style guide at `skill://style-guard` (and `literature/style-guide.md` in the project if present)
3. Run the plagiarism detection analysis
4. Run the AI-artifact detection analysis
5. Run the style consistency analysis
6. Produce a consolidated report with findings, severity ratings, and rewrite suggestions
7. Update the style guide if new patterns are observed

1. **Integrity over convenience.** If text passes automated checks but still feels derivative or machine-assisted, flag it. A false negative is worse than a false positive.
2. **Preserve meaning, change form.** When rewriting, never alter the substantive content — only the way it is expressed.
3. **Respect the author's voice.** Your style guide captures how this writer actually writes. Deviations from that voice are as suspicious as deviations from source material.
4. **Evidence-based judgment.** Every flag must cite a specific pattern, not a vague impression. "Feels AI-generated" is not a finding. "Three consecutive paragraphs with identical 45-word counts and gerund-phrase openers" is a finding.
5. **Graduated response.** Not every artifact requires a rewrite. Minor issues get noted; patterns get flagged; systemic problems get full rewrites with explanations.

## When Invoked

1. Read the provided text in full
2. Check the style guide at `skill://style-guard` (and `literature/style-guide.md` in the project if present)
3. Run the plagiarism detection analysis
4. Run the AI-artifact detection analysis
5. Run the style consistency analysis
6. Produce a consolidated report with findings, severity ratings, and rewrite suggestions
7. Update the style guide if new patterns are observed

---

truncation.content:
---
name: res-phd-literature-retriever
description: "Use when you need to retrieve and download academic papers — search Anna's Archive for full texts, organize downloads into the project's literature directory, and maintain a local library of referenced works."
model: ollama-cloud/devstral-small-2:24b
tools: [read, write, edit, bash, find, search]
---

You are an academic literature retrieval specialist. Your job is to find and download full-text academic papers and organize them into the project's literature library. You work as part of a research pipeline: you receive retrieval requests from `res-phd-literature-searcher` and hand off downloaded papers to `res-phd-literature-reviewer`.

## Core Principles

- **Accuracy over speed.** Downloading the wrong paper is worse than downloading nothing — it contaminates the literature library and wastes downstream review time.
- **Verify every download.** Never report a paper as retrieved until you have confirmed the file is a valid PDF containing the expected content.
- **Respect sources.** Follow rate limits. Do not hammer any endpoint. Pause between retries.
- **Report honestly.** If a paper is behind a paywall, behind a CAPTCHA, or unavailable, say so. Never fabricate a download.

## Directory Structure

```
literature/
  papers/
    <author>-<year>-<short-title>.pdf
  citations.md
```

File naming convention: `<author1surname>-<year>-<short-key>.pdf`
- Author surname: lowercase, hyphens for multi-part names (e.g. `van-der-berg`)
- Year: 4-digit publication year
- Short key: 2-4 word hyphenated slug derived from the title (e.g. `bayesian-inference` for "Bayesian Inference in Latent Variable Models")
- Examples: `gelman-2013-bayesian-data.pdf`, `pearl-2009-causality.pdf`, `blei-2003-latent-dirichlet.pdf`

## Download Workflow

For each paper requested, execute this sequence:

### Step 1: Check local library

```bash
# Check if already downloaded
ls literature/papers/ | grep -i "<author>.*<year>.*<key>"
```

If the file exists and passes integrity check (see Verification below), report it as already available. Skip to Step 6.

### Step 2: Search Anna's Archive

```bash
# Search by title
curl -s -L "https://annas-archive.org/search?q=$(python3 -c "import urllib.parse; print(urllib.parse.quote('<title>'))")" \
  -o /tmp/anna-search.html

All done. Here's the complete PhD research assistant system:

---

## 9 PhD Research Agents

| Agent | Model | Role | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `res-phd-academic-writer` | MiniMax M2.7 | `long-document` | Writing, revising, polishing academic text |
| `res-phd-peer-reviewer` | DeepSeek V4 Pro | `deep-audit` | Rigorous constructive critique |
| `res-phd-literature-searcher` | Qwen3.5:397b | `research-synthesis` | Search scholarly databases, maintain citations.md |
| `res-phd-plagiarism-guard` | DeepSeek V4 Pro | `deep-audit` | Plagiarism risk, AI artifacts, style guide |
| `res-phd-literature-retriever` | Devstral Small 2:24b | `fast-coding` | Anna's Archive + arXiv/Semantic Scholar downloads |
| `res-phd-literature-reviewer` | Qwen3.5:397b | `research-synthesis` | Read papers, synthesize, write lit reviews |
| `res-phd-research-methodologist` | Kimi K2.6 | `architecture-planning` | Research design, validity, ethics, reproducibility |
| `res-phd-data-analyst` | Qwen3 Coder Next | `code-specialist` | Statistics, visualization, results reporting |
| `res-phd-citation-formatter` | DeepSeek V4 Flash | `fast-reasoning` | APA/IEEE/Chicago/Vancouver/Harvard/MLA compliance |

## 4 PhD Skills

| Skill | Lines | Used by |
|---|---|---|
| `academic-writing` | 415 | academic-writer, peer-reviewer, methodologist |
| `literature-search` | 562 | literature-searcher, literature-reviewer |
| `style-guard` | 480 | plagiarism-guard, academic-writer |
| `literature-retrieval` | 377 | literature-retriever |

## Research Pipeline

```
[Search] res-phd-literature-searcher → finds papers across 5 databases
   ↓
[Retrieve] res-phd-literature-retriever → downloads from Anna's Archive / arXiv / Unpaywall
   ↓                          ↓
[Review] res-phd-literature-reviewer → reads, synthesizes, identifies gaps
   ↓
[Write] res-phd-academic-writer → drafts thesis chapters/papers
   ↓
[Review] res-phd-peer-reviewer → challenges arguments, suggests improvements
   ↓
[Guard] res-phd-plagiarism-guard → checks originality, AI artifacts, style
   ↓
[Format] res-phd-citation-formatter → ensures citation style compliance
```

Supporting agents: `res-phd-research-methodologist` (design), `res-phd-data-analyst` (analysis).

## Project Structure Convention

```
<project>/
  literature/
    papers/          # Downloaded PDFs
    notes/           # Reading notes per paper
    citations.md     # Structured citation database
    style-guide.md   # Your personal writing style
```

- **role-map.yml**: 9 new agent→role entries under "PhD Research Assistants" section
- **manifest.yml**: New `[sources.custom-agents]` section tracking the 9 agents; 4 new skills added to `[sources.custom]`
- **xpert extension**: Added `phd` category (first in CATEGORY_ORDER) so `/xpert` shows PhD assistants at the top

 6ar|	"bash-tool-runtime.md": "# Bash tool runtime

This document describes the **`bash` tool** runtime path used by agent tool calls, from command normalization to execution, truncation/artifacts, and rendering.

It also calls out where behavior diverges in interactive TUI, print mode, RPC mode, and user-initiated bang (`!`) shell execution.

## Scope and runtime surfaces

There are two different bash execution surfaces in coding-agent:

1. **Tool-call surface** (`toolName: "bash"`): used when the model calls the bash tool.
   - Entry point: `BashTool.execute()`.
   - Parameters include `command`, optional `env`, `timeout`, `cwd`, `head`, `tail`, `pty`, and, when `async.enabled` is true, `async`.
2. **User bang-command surface** (`!cmd` from interactive input or RPC `bash` command): session-level helper path.
   - Entry point: `AgentSession.executeBash()`.

Both eventually use `executeBash()` in `src/exec/bash-executor.ts` for non-PTY execution, but only the tool-call path runs normalization/...
*7tn|	"blob-artifact-architecture.md": "# Blob and artifact storage architecture

This document describes how coding-agent stores large/binary payloads outside session JSONL, how truncated tool output is persisted, and how internal URLs (`artifact://`, `agent://`) resolve back to stored data.

## Why two storage systems exist

The runtime uses two different persistence mechanisms for different data shapes:

- **Content-addressed blobs** (`blob:sha256:<hash>`): global storage used to externalize large image base64 payloads and provider image data URLs from persisted session entries.
- **Session-scoped artifacts** (files under `<sessionFile-without-.jsonl>/`): per-session text files used for full tool outputs and subagent outputs.

They are intentionally separate:

- blob storage optimizes deduplication and stable references by content hash,
- artifact storage optimizes append-only session tooling and human/tool retrieval by local IDs.

## Storage boundaries and on-disk layout

 8gs|	"compaction.md": "# Compaction and Branch Summaries

Compaction and branch summaries are the two mechanisms that keep long sessions usable without losing prior work context.

- **Compaction** rewrites old history into a summary on the current branch.
- **Branch summary** captures abandoned branch context during `/tree` navigation.

Both are persisted as session entries and converted back into user-context messages when rebuilding LLM input.

*10qq|	"custom-tools.md": "# Custom Tools

Custom tools are model-callable functions that plug into the same tool execution pipeline as built-in tools.

A custom tool is a TypeScript/JavaScript module that exports a factory. The factory receives a host API (`CustomToolAPI`) and returns one tool or an array of tools.

## What this is (and is not)

- **Extension**: lifecycle/event framework that can register tools and intercept/modify events.
- **Hook**: external pre/post command scripts.
- **Skill**: static guidance/context package, not executable tool code.

If you need the model to call code directly, use a custom tool.

There are two active integration styles:

This is a practical guide for moving hot paths into `crates/pi-natives` and wiring them through the generated native package entrypoint. It exists to avoid the same failures happening twice.

## When to port

Port when any of these are true:

- The hot path runs in render loops, tight UI updates, or large batches.
- JS allocations dominate (string churn, regex backtracking, large arrays).
- You already have a JS baseline and can benchmark both versions side by side.
- The work is CPU-bound or blocking I/O that can run on the libuv thread pool.
- The work is async I/O that can run on Tokio's runtime (for example shell execution).

Avoid ports that depend on JS-only state or dynamic imports. N-API exports should be data-in/data-out. Long-running work should go through `task::blocking` (CPU-bound/blocking I/O) or `task::future` (async I/O) with cancellation where the caller needs `timeoutMs` or `AbortSignal`.

 44dg|	"secrets.md": "# Secret Obfuscation

Prevents sensitive values (API keys, tokens, passwords) from being sent to LLM providers. When enabled, secrets are replaced with deterministic placeholders before leaving the process, and restored in tool call arguments returned by the model.

## Enabling

Disabled by default. Toggle via `/settings` UI or directly in `config.yml`:

```yaml
secrets:
  enabled: true
```

## How it works

1. On session startup, secrets are collected from two sources:
   - **Environment variables** whose names match common secret patterns (`KEY`, `SECRET`, `TOKEN`, `PASSWORD`, `PASS`, `AUTH`, `CREDENTIAL`, `PRIVATE`, `OAUTH`) with values >= 8 characters
   - **`secrets.yml` files** (see below)

2. Outbound text messages to the LLM have secret values replaced with deterministic placeholders like `#AB12#`.

3. Session context/tool arguments returned from the model are deep-walked and obfuscation placeholders are restored to original values before display or execution.

 *7│	"blob-artifact-architecture.md": "# Blob and artifact storage architecture

This document describes how coding-agent stores large/binary payloads outside session JSONL, how truncated tool output is persisted, and how internal URLs (`artifact://`, `agent://`) resolve back to stored data.

## Why two storage systems exist

The runtime uses two different persistence mechanisms for different data shapes:

- **Content-addressed blobs** (`blob:sha256:<hash>`): global storage used to externalize large image base64 payloads and provider image data URLs from persisted session entries.
- **Session-scoped artifacts** (files under `<sessionFile-without-.jsonl>/`): per-session text files used for full tool outputs and subagent outputs.

They are intentionally separate:

- blob storage optimizes deduplication and stable references by content hash,
- artifact storage optimizes append-only session tooling and human/tool retrieval by local IDs.

Custom tools are model-callable functions that plug into the same tool execution pipeline as built-in tools.

A custom tool is a TypeScript/JavaScript module that exports a factory. The factory receives a host API (`CustomToolAPI`) and returns one tool or an array of tools.

## What this is (and is not)

- **Extension**: lifecycle/event framework that can register tools and intercept/modify events.
- **Hook**: external pre/post command scripts.
- **Skill**: static guidance/context package, not executable tool code.

If you need the model to call code directly, use a custom tool.

When enabled, the agent automatically extracts durable knowledge from past sessions and injects a compact summary into each new session. Over time it builds a project-scoped memory store — technical decisions, recurring workflows, pitfalls — that carries forward without manual effort.

Disabled by default. Enable via `/settings` or `config.yml`:

memories:
  enabled: true
```

## Usage

### What gets injected

At session start, if a memory summary exists for the current project, it is injected into the system prompt as a **Memory Guidance** block. The agent is instructed to:

- Treat memory as heuristic context — useful for process and prior decisions, not authoritative on current repo state.
- Cite the memory artifact path when memory changes the plan, and pair it with current-repo evidence before acting.
- Prefer repo state and user instruction when they conflict with memory; treat conflicting memory as stale.

This is a practical guide for moving hot paths into `crates/pi-natives` and wiring them through the generated native package entrypoint. It exists to avoid the same failures happening twice.

## When to port

Port when any of these are true:

- The hot path runs in render loops, tight UI updates, or large batches.
- JS allocations dominate (string churn, regex backtracking, large arrays).
- You already have a JS baseline and can benchmark both versions side by side.
- The work is CPU-bound or blocking I/O that can run on the libuv thread pool.
- The work is async I/O that can run on Tokio's runtime (for example shell execution).

Avoid ports that depend on JS-only state or dynamic imports. N-API exports should be data-in/data-out. Long-running work should go through `task::blocking` (CPU-bound/blocking I/O) or `task::future` (async I/O) with cancellation where the caller needs `timeoutMs` or `AbortSignal`.

 44│	"secrets.md": "# Secret Obfuscation

Prevents sensitive values (API keys, tokens, passwords) from being sent to LLM providers. When enabled, secrets are replaced with deterministic placeholders before leaving the process, and restored in tool call arguments returned by the model.

## Enabling

Disabled by default. Toggle via `/settings` UI or directly in `config.yml`:

```yaml
secrets:
  enabled: true
```

## How it works

1. On session startup, secrets are collected from two sources:
   - **Environment variables** whose names match common secret patterns (`KEY`, `SECRET`, `TOKEN`, `PASSWORD`, `PASS`, `AUTH`, `CREDENTIAL`, `PRIVATE`, `OAUTH`) with values >= 8 characters
   - **`secrets.yml` files** (see below)

2. Outbound text messages to the LLM have secret values replaced with deterministic placeholders like `#AB12#`.

3. Session context/tool arguments returned from the model are deep-walked and obfuscation placeholders are restored to original values before display or execution.

content.text: Now I see it. Let me read the extension module discovery code more carefully:

Now I see the problem. There are TWO different extension systems:

1. **Gemini-style manifest extensions** (`gemini-extension.json`) — discovered from `~/.omp/agent/extensions/<name>/gemini-extension.json`
2. **TypeScript/JS extension modules** — discovered from `package.json` `omp.extensions` field or from `settings.json`

The xpert extension has a `package.json` with `omp.extensions` but no `gemini-extension.json`. Let me check how extension modules are discovered:

Now I see the discovery flow. The xpert extension should be found because:

1. It has `package.json` in the subdirectory with `omp.extensions: ["./index.ts"]`
2. The discovery reads `package.json`, finds declared extensions, resolves `./index.ts`

The ones NOT in the list are enabled by default. That's the opposite of what we want — the user said "all agents are enabled" meaning they want most disabled by default.

The user wants agents to be on-demand only (activated via `/xpert`), not auto-loaded. The `disabledAgents` list needs to include ALL agents except the ones the user wants active by default.

*49tm|	"skills.md": "# Skills

Skills are file-backed capability packs discovered at startup and exposed to the model as:

- lightweight metadata in the system prompt (name + description)
- on-demand content via `read skill://...`
- optional interactive `/skill:<name>` commands

This document covers current runtime behavior in `src/extensibility/skills.ts`, `src/discovery/builtin.ts`, `src/internal-urls/skill-protocol.ts`, and `src/discovery/agents-md.ts`.

## What a skill is in this codebase

A discovered skill is represented as:

- `name`
- `description`
- `filePath` (the `SKILL.md` path)
- `baseDir` (skill directory)
- source metadata (`provider`, `level`, path)

The runtime only requires `name` and `path` for validity. In practice, matching quality depends on `description` being meaningful.

## Required layout and SKILL.md expectations

### Directory layout

For provider-based discovery (native/Claude/Codex/Agents/plugin providers), skills are discovered as **one level under `skills...

description: Use when creating a new omp extension. Covers ExtensionAPI, factory signature, tool/command/event registration, and local-dev testing.
---

# Authoring Extensions

Extensions are the primary way to add capabilities to `oh-my-pi`. A single extension module can register tools the LLM can call, slash commands users can invoke, and event handlers that run throughout the session lifecycle — all from one TypeScript file.

## Minimum viable extension

```ts
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.on("session_start", async (_event, ctx) => {
    ctx.ui.notify("My extension loaded!", "info");
  });
}
```

That is a working extension. Drop it into `~/.omp/agent/extensions/hello.ts` and restart omp to see the notification.

name: authoring-hooks
description: Use when creating a new omp hook. Covers HookAPI, event catalog, blocking/overriding tool calls, and context modification.
---

# Authoring Hooks

Hooks are event-driven interceptors that run alongside the agent loop. They are best used for cross-cutting concerns: safety policy, secret redaction, context pruning, audit logging. A hook module registers handlers via `pi.on(event, handler)` and can block tool execution, override tool output, or rewrite the message context before each LLM call.

> **Relationship to extensions:** The hook subsystem (`HookAPI`) is the legacy API. The extension runner now handles everything hooks can do plus more. `ExtensionAPI` supports the hook event model plus extension-only events. Use `ExtensionAPI` for new work; use `HookAPI` only if you are maintaining an existing hook module.

## Factory signature

```ts
import type { HookAPI } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent/extensibility/hooks";

# Authoring Marketplaces

A marketplace is a Git repository (or local directory) that contains a catalog file at `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`. Anyone can author one. Users add it with `/marketplace add owner/repo` and then install individual plugins from it.

## Minimum viable marketplace

```
my-marketplace/
  .claude-plugin/
    marketplace.json
  plugins/
    my-plugin/
      package.json
      index.ts
```

```json
{
  "name": "my-marketplace",
  "owner": { "name": "Your Name" },
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "my-plugin",
      "description": "What it does",
      "source": "./plugins/my-plugin"
    }
  ]
}
```

Push to GitHub. Users install with:

```
/marketplace add your-github-username/my-marketplace

RPC mode runs the coding agent as a newline-delimited JSON protocol over stdio.

- **stdin**: commands (`RpcCommand`), extension UI responses, and host-tool updates/results
- **stdout**: a ready frame, command responses (`RpcResponse`), session/agent events, extension UI requests, host-tool requests/cancellations

Primary implementation:

- `src/modes/rpc/rpc-mode.ts`
- `src/modes/rpc/rpc-types.ts`
- `src/session/agent-session.ts`
- `packages/agent/src/agent.ts`
- `packages/agent/src/agent-loop.ts`

## Startup

```bash
omp --mode rpc [regular CLI options]
```

Behavior notes:

- `@file` CLI arguments are rejected in RPC mode.
- RPC mode disables automatic session title generation by default to avoid an extra model call.
- RPC mode resets workflow-altering `todo.*`, `task.*`, `async.*`, and `bash.autoBackground.*` settings to their built-in defaults instead of inheriting user overrides.

It reflects the current implementation, including partial semantics and metadata that is parsed but not enforced.

 44│	"secrets.md": "# Secret Obfuscation

Prevents sensitive values (API keys, tokens, passwords) from being sent to LLM providers. When enabled, secrets are replaced with deterministic placeholders before leaving the process, and restored in tool call arguments returned by the model.

## Enabling

Disabled by default. Toggle via `/settings` UI or directly in `config.yml`:

```yaml
secrets:
  enabled: true
```

## How it works

1. On session startup, secrets are collected from two sources:
   - **Environment variables** whose names match common secret patterns (`KEY`, `SECRET`, `TOKEN`, `PASSWORD`, `PASS`, `AUTH`, `CREDENTIAL`, `PRIVATE`, `OAUTH`) with values >= 8 characters
   - **`secrets.yml` files** (see below)

2. Outbound text messages to the LLM have secret values replaced with deterministic placeholders like `#AB12#`.

3. Session context/tool arguments returned from the model are deep-walked and obfuscation placeholders are restored to original values before display or execution.

*49│	"skills.md": "# Skills

Skills are file-backed capability packs discovered at startup and exposed to the model as:

- lightweight metadata in the system prompt (name + description)
- on-demand content via `read skill://...`
- optional interactive `/skill:<name>` commands

This document covers current runtime behavior in `src/extensibility/skills.ts`, `src/discovery/builtin.ts`, `src/internal-urls/skill-protocol.ts`, and `src/discovery/agents-md.ts`.

## What a skill is in this codebase

A discovered skill is represented as:

- `name`
- `description`
- `filePath` (the `SKILL.md` path)
- `baseDir` (skill directory)
- source metadata (`provider`, `level`, path)

The runtime only requires `name` and `path` for validity. In practice, matching quality depends on `description` being meaningful.

## Required layout and SKILL.md expectations

For provider-based discovery (native/Claude/Codex/Agents/plugin providers), skills are discovered as **one level under `skills...

name: authoring-extensions
description: Use when creating a new omp extension. Covers ExtensionAPI, factory signature, tool/command/event registration, and local-dev testing.
---

# Authoring Extensions

Extensions are the primary way to add capabilities to `oh-my-pi`. A single extension module can register tools the LLM can call, slash commands users can invoke, and event handlers that run throughout the session lifecycle — all from one TypeScript file.

## Minimum viable extension

```ts
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.on("session_start", async (_event, ctx) => {
    ctx.ui.notify("My extension loaded!", "info");
  });
}
```

That is a working extension. Drop it into `~/.omp/agent/extensions/hello.ts` and restart omp to see the notification.

 51│	"skills/authoring-hooks.md": "---
name: authoring-hooks
description: Use when creating a new omp hook. Covers HookAPI, event catalog, blocking/overriding tool calls, and context modification.
---

# Authoring Hooks

Hooks are event-driven interceptors that run alongside the agent loop. They are best used for cross-cutting concerns: safety policy, secret redaction, context pruning, audit logging. A hook module registers handlers via `pi.on(event, handler)` and can block tool execution, override tool output, or rewrite the message context before each LLM call.

> **Relationship to extensions:** The hook subsystem (`HookAPI`) is the legacy API. The extension runner now handles everything hooks can do plus more. `ExtensionAPI` supports the hook event model plus extension-only events. Use `ExtensionAPI` for new work; use `HookAPI` only if you are maintaining an existing hook module.

# Authoring Marketplaces

A marketplace is a Git repository (or local directory) that contains a catalog file at `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`. Anyone can author one. Users add it with `/marketplace add owner/repo` and then install individual plugins from it.

## Minimum viable marketplace

```
my-marketplace/
  .claude-plugin/
    marketplace.json
  plugins/
    my-plugin/
      package.json
      index.ts
```

```json
{
  "name": "my-marketplace",
  "owner": { "name": "Your Name" },
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "my-plugin",
      "description": "What it does",
      "source": "./plugins/my-plugin"
    }
  ]
}
```

Push to GitHub. Users install with:

```
/marketplace add your-github-username/my-marketplace
/marketplace install...

---

## Two bugs fixed

### 1. `/xpert` command not working

**Cause**: Syntax error in `~/.omp/agent/extensions/xpert/index.ts` line 416 — mismatched parentheses:
```ts
// BROKEN:
const scriptPath = path.join(path.dirname(new URL(import.meta.url).pathname)), "reconcile-agents.py");
```
The `path.join()` call was closed prematurely, and `"reconcile-agents.py"` was a stray argument. The line was also dead code since the actual script path was on the next line.

**Fix**: Removed the broken line. The reconcile command already used the direct path to the script on the next line. Extension now builds and imports cleanly.

### 2. All agents enabled in `/agent` dashboard

**Cause**: `~/.omp/agent/config.yml` had an incomplete `disabledAgents` list — only ~70 agents were listed, missing all `dev-*`, `lang-*`, `infra-*`, `dx-*`, `meta-*` agents and the new `res-phd-*` agents. OMP shows all agents not in this list as "enabled" by default.

**Fix**: Updated `config.yml` to include all 153 custom agents plus `explore`, `plan`, `reviewer`, `quick_task` in `disabledAgents`. The built-in `task`, `designer`, and `librarian` agents remain enabled since they're core OMP functionality.

**Result**: All custom agents now show as disabled in the `/agent` dashboard. Activate them on-demand via `/xpert`.

The user is asking two things:

1. **About the disabled built-in agents**: They want to know if `explore`, `plan`, `reviewer`, `quick_task` are core OMP functionality that should stay enabled.

2. **A better `/xpert` command**: They want a TUI-style interface with:

   - hjkl/arrow navigation
   - Space to toggle individual agents
   - Some key to toggle entire groups
   - Presets like "Python project", "Documentation", "Shipping/NixOS" that activate relevant agent+skill teams

Let me think about the built-in agents first:
- `explore` - OMP's built-in codebase scout
- `plan` - OMP's architectural planning agent
- `reviewer` - OMP's code reviewer
- `quick_task` - Low-reasoning agent for mechanical tasks
- `task` - Core task delegation agent
- `designer` - UI/UX designer
- `librarian` - Library researcher

`explore` and `task` are heavily used by OMP's internal `task` tool for spawning subagents. Disabling `explore` and `quick_task` would break the `task` tool's ability to use those agent types. Same with `plan` and `reviewer`.

So I should re-enable `explore`, `plan`, `reviewer`, and `quick_task` since they're used by OMP's `task` delegation system.

Now for the `/xpert` redesign. The current implementation uses `ctx.ui.select()` which is a simple single-choice selector. What the user wants is something much more interactive — a TUI with:
- Browse agents by category/group
- Toggle individual agents with space
- Toggle entire groups with a key
- Presets that activate teams of agents+skills
- hjkl/arrow navigation

The problem is that OMP extensions register commands via `pi.registerCommand()`, and the handler receives `(args, ctx)`. The `ctx.ui.select()` is the only interactive UI method available. There's no TUI component API for building custom interfaces.

However, I can approach this differently:
1. Instead of a TUI, use a command-based approach with subcommands
2. `/xpert` - show presets and let user pick one
3. `/xpert toggle <group>` - toggle a group
4. `/xpert activate <preset>` - activate a preset
5. `/xpert on <group>` / `/xpert off <group>`

But the user specifically wants a keyboard-driven interactive experience. Let me think about what OMP's extension API actually supports...

Looking at the extension types, `ctx.ui.select()` and `ctx.ui.notify()` are the main UI methods. There might also be `ctx.ui.confirm()` or similar. But for a full TUI with hjkl navigation, we'd need a custom TUI component.

Actually, looking at the agent-dashboard.ts code, OMP already has a TUI component for the agent dashboard. But extensions can't create custom TUI components — they're limited to the `ExtensionAPI` interface.

Let me also think about what happens with skills. Skills have `alwaysApply: false`, which means they're only activated when explicitly loaded. The `/xpert skill <name>` command loads them into context. For presets, I should also activate the paired skills.

Actually, looking at skills more carefully — skills with `alwaysApply: false` are "on-demand" skills that the model can read via `skill://name` URLs. They don't need to be "enabled" in the same way agents do. They're always discoverable but not loaded into context unless the model reads them or the user uses `/xpert skill <name>`.

So for presets, the key action is:
1. Enable the relevant agents in config.yml (remove from disabledAgents)
2. Notify the user which skills are paired

## Fixed

### 1. `/xpert` command — syntax error fixed
The extension had a broken `path.join()` call on line 416. Fixed by removing the dead code line. Extension now builds and imports cleanly.

### 2. Built-in agents — re-enabled
`explore`, `plan`, `reviewer`, `quick_task` were incorrectly in `disabledAgents`. These are core OMP agents used by the `task` tool internally. They're now enabled. The full list of built-in agents that remain enabled:

| Agent | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `task` | Core task delegation (used by `/task` command) |
| `quick_task` | Low-reasoning mechanical tasks |
| `explore` | Codebase scouting |
| `plan` | Architectural planning |
| `designer` | UI/UX design |
| `reviewer` | Code review |
| `librarian` | Library research |

### 3. `/xpert` redesigned as team-based manager

The `/xpert` command is now a **team browser and manager**:

**Presets** (10 teams, each activating a curated set of agents + paired skills):

| Team | Agents | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| 🎓 PhD Research Team | 9 research agents | Literature, writing, review, citation |
| 🐍 Python Development | 7 agents | Python backend/AI/data |
| 🦀 Rust Development | 3 agents | Systems programming |
| ⚛️ TypeScript & Frontend | 6 agents | TS/React/Next.js |
| 🐹 Go Development | 3 agents | Go backend |
| 🚢 DevOps & Shipping | 6 agents | Docker, Terraform, K8s, SRE |
| 🔒 Security & Code Review | 5 agents | Security audit, penetration testing |
| 📝 Documentation & Writing | 6 agents | Docs, academic writing, peer review |
| 📊 Data Science & ML | 7 agents | Statistics, ML, NLP |
| 🌐 Full-Stack Web App | 7 agents | End-to-end web development |

**Commands:**

| Command | Action |
|---|---|
| `/xpert` | Interactive: shows team overview with activation bars, pick a team to view details and activate/deactivate |
| `/xpert on <team>` | Activate all agents in a team |
| `/xpert off <team>` | Deactivate all agents in a team |
| `/xpert toggle <prefix>` | Toggle all agents with that prefix (e.g., `toggle res-phd`) |
| `/xpert status` | Show all teams with activation status bars |
| `/xpert list` | Show all 154 agents by category |
| `/xpert skills` | Show all skills |
| `/xpert models` | Show model catalog |

The visualization shows each team with a progress bar (`████░░░ 7/9`), agent-by-agent status with model names and descriptions, and paired skill references. Activating a team modifies `config.yml`'s `disabledAgents` list directly, so changes persist and the `/agent` dashboard reflects them immediately.

content.text:
Now I understand the TUI infrastructure. The `AgentDashboard` extends `Container` from `@oh-my-pi/pi-tui` and uses `handleInput()`, `matchesKey()`, `Component`, `Text`, `Spacer`, `Input`, etc. to build a full interactive TUI with keyboard navigation.

The extension API (`ctx.ui.select()`, `ctx.ui.notify()`, `ctx.ui.confirm()`) is a simpler interface — it can't build custom TUI components. But extensions CAN use `pi.on("session_start", ...)` and they have access to `ctx.ui.select()` which provides a simple selector dialog.

The key insight: I can't build a full hjkl-driven TUI dashboard inside an extension. That requires a native TUI component like `AgentDashboard` which is part of OMP's internal rendering system.

However, I can build a much better `/xpert` experience using the available tools:
1. **Multi-level `ctx.ui.select()`** — hierarchical navigation through categories → teams → agents
2. **`pi.sendMessage()`** for rich visualization panels showing team composition with status
3. **`ctx.ui.onTerminalInput()`** for custom key handling in interactive mode

But `onTerminalInput()` only works in interactive mode and doesn't give us a rendered TUI surface — it just lets us intercept keypresses. We can't render our own TUI grid.

The realistic approach is to make the `/xpert` experience as fast and fluid as possible with the available API:
- Show a rich visualization panel with team status
- Use `ctx.ui.select()` for hierarchical navigation
- Support quick commands like `/xpert on phd-research` for one-shot activation
- Support `/xpert toggle res-phd` for prefix-based toggling

And for the "150+ agents and only a few templates" problem — I need to create more teams/presets that cover all the categories. Let me define teams for every major workflow.

Let me also check if there's a way to create a custom TUI component as an extension:

The `pi-tui` package exports `Container`, `Component`, `Text`, `Input`, etc. Extensions import from `@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent` which re-exports these. But extensions can only register tools, commands, and event handlers — they can't inject TUI components into the agent dashboard.

So the best I can do is a really good multi-level selector experience. Let me design this carefully.

The user wants:
1. Hierarchical "folder" navigation
2. hjkl/arrow key support
3. Space to toggle individual agents
4. Key to toggle entire group
5. Presets/templates for common workflows
6. More teams — we have 150+ agents and only 10 teams

For the hierarchical navigation with `ctx.ui.select()`:
- Level 1: Choose a category/folder (phd, code, infra, etc.)
- Level 2: Choose a team within that category
- Level 3: Choose action (activate, deactivate, view details, toggle individual agents)

For more teams, I need to cover all 150+ agents with meaningful presets. Let me think about what makes sense:

**By Language/Stack:**
- Python Dev (7 agents)
- Rust Dev (3 agents)
- TypeScript/Frontend Dev (6 agents)
- Go Dev (3 agents)
- Java/Spring Dev (3 agents)
- C/C++ Dev (2 agents)
- C#/.NET Dev (3 agents)
- Ruby/Rails Dev (2 agents)
- Elixir Dev (1 agent)
- PHP/Laravel Dev (2 agents)
- Mobile Dev (3 agents)

**By Workflow:**
- PhD Research (9 agents)
- DevOps/Shipping (6 agents)
- Security Review (5 agents)
- Documentation (6 agents)
- Data Science/ML (7 agents)
- Full-Stack Web (7 agents)
- Code Quality (5 agents)
- Architecture (5 agents)
- Cloud Infrastructure (4 agents)

**By Role:**
- Startup CTO (5 agents — backend + frontend + devops + security)
- Solo Dev (3 agents — fullstack + code review + devops)
- Student/Researcher (9 agents — PhD team)

This gives us ~25-30 teams covering all 150+ agents.

Actually, I think the right approach is different. Instead of pre-defined teams, let me build a **folder-based browser** where:

1. Categories are the "folders" (same as the agent prefixes: phd, dev, lang, infra, qual, data, dx, spec, biz, meta, res)
2. Each folder shows all agents in that category with their status
3. You can toggle individual agents with a selection or toggle the whole group
4. Presets/teams are additional quick-activate shortcuts

The user wants hjkl navigation. `ctx.ui.select()` uses arrow keys (or j/k in some implementations). It's not a full TUI, but it's the best we can do with the extension API.

Let me implement this. The key improvement over the current version:
1. More teams (covering all agents)
2. Hierarchical navigation: folders → teams → agents
3. Rich visualization panel showing everything
4. Quick commands for common operations

Let me write the new extension. This is going to be substantial but I'll make it thorough.

Actually, let me reconsider the architecture. The user explicitly said "organized hierarchically in folders that I can navigate with hjkl". The `ctx.ui.select()` function IS navigable with arrow keys/j/k. So I can build a multi-level selector:

1. First level: Choose what to do (Browse Teams, Browse Categories, Toggle Prefix, Status)
2. If Browse Teams: select a team → activate/deactivate/view
3. If Browse Categories: select a category → see agents with status → toggle individual/group
4. If Toggle Prefix: enter a prefix → toggle all matching agents

For the "folder" metaphor, I can use categories as folders. Each category contains its agents, and you can toggle them on/off.

Let me also think about the visualization. The `pi.sendMessage()` with `display: true` shows markdown content. I can create rich tables showing team composition, activation status, etc.
